name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage where users start a Hawaii vacation rental search by selecting destination, dates, guests, and pets, with shortcuts for common destinations and date ranges and access to account and wishlist entry points.

core_features: Load header user summary to show account presence and wishlist indicator using getHeaderUserSummary.
Load homepage configuration (featured destinations, quick date ranges, default guest/pet counts, profile presence) using getHomePageConfig.
Allow user to search by destination, check-in/check-out dates, number of adults, children, and pets, and submit the search via a main search form.
Provide destination autocomplete suggestions while typing, using getDestinationSuggestions, and allow selecting one as the search destination.
Provide quick date range shortcuts (e.g., predefined stay lengths) based on quickDateRanges from getHomePageConfig that pre-fill the date range in the search form.
Provide featured destination shortcuts based on featuredDestinations from getHomePageConfig that pre-fill the destination in the search form.
Trigger a new search via startNewSearch when the main search form is submitted, creating a SearchContext and then navigating to search_results.html with search parameters encoded in the URL.
Provide account entry actions (sign in / sign up / profile) via the header, including navigation to profile.html with the required URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: New visitor starts a basic search
1) User navigates to index.html (no query parameters required).
2) On page load, the application calls getHeaderUserSummary and getHomePageConfig in parallel.
3) Header is populated with account presence and wishlist count from getHeaderUserSummary.
4) Main search form is initialized with default guest counts from getHomePageConfig.defaultGuests; destination and dates are empty.
5) User clicks into the destination input and types text.
6) After each change (with debouncing), destination-autocomplete component calls getDestinationSuggestions(query).
7) User selects a suggestion from the list; the selected Destination object (id, name, etc.) is bound to the search form state.
8) User opens the date range picker and selects check-in and check-out dates; the component validates date order and updates the form state.
9) User adjusts adults, children, pets counts in the guest/pets selector.
10) User clicks the search form submit button.
11) Form validation runs: ensures a destination is selected from suggestions and dates are valid and complete; if invalid, inline errors are shown and submission is blocked.
12) If valid, the page calls startNewSearch(destinationId, checkIn, checkOut, adultsCount, childrenCount, petsCount) using the current form state.
13) On success, startNewSearch returns a SearchContext and an initial results array; the home page does not render the results but uses the SearchContext data.
14) The application navigates to search_results.html, encoding at minimum destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and pets as URL parameters, and may also include the SearchContext id as an additional URL parameter.
15) The search_results.html page can then use the URL parameters to reconstruct or continue the search.

Workflow 2: Using quick date ranges
1) On page load, getHomePageConfig returns quickDateRanges.
2) The quick-date-range-selector component renders a list of options using the labels from quickDateRanges.
3) With the search form visible, the user clicks one of the quick date range options.
4) The component computes a check-in and check-out date range based on the clicked option and the current date (or other defined logic), then updates the shared search form's date state.
5) The user can optionally adjust dates manually in the date picker; changes override the quick date selection.
6) The user fills destination and guest/pet counts if not already set.
7) The user submits the search; startNewSearch is called and navigation to search_results.html occurs as in Workflow 1.

Workflow 3: Using featured destination shortcuts
1) On page load, getHomePageConfig returns featuredDestinations.
2) The featured-destination-shortcuts component renders one interactive element per featured destination, each bound to the respective destinationId and destinationName.
3) User clicks a featured destination shortcut.
4) The main search form's destination state is set to the clicked destination (Destination id/name/type/slug); the destination input reflects this selection.
5) The user then selects dates (via quick date ranges or manual date picker) and adjusts guest/pet counts.
6) User submits the search, which triggers startNewSearch and then navigation to search_results.html with URL parameters based on the chosen destination and form state.

Workflow 4: Returning user with prefilled search via URL
1) User reaches index.html with search parameters present in the URL, such as destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets.
2) On page initialization, a URL parsing routine reads these parameters.
3) If destinationId and destinationName are present, the main search form initializes its destination state to these values without calling getDestinationSuggestions; the destination input displays the provided destinationName.
4) If checkIn and checkOut are present, the date-range-picker component initializes its internal state with these dates, provided they form a valid range; otherwise they are ignored and the picker starts empty.
5) If adults, children, or pets parameters are present, the guest/pets selector initializes counts from the URL values instead of getHomePageConfig.defaultGuests.
6) User reviews or adjusts the prefilled values and submits the search.
7) startNewSearch is called with the potentially updated values and navigation to search_results.html occurs as in Workflow 1.

Workflow 5: Account entry from the home page
1) On page load, getHeaderUserSummary and getHomePageConfig indicate whether a profile exists.
2) Header account area shows:
   - if hasProfile is true: a profile/menu control with profileName, and a wishlist indicator using defaultWishlistRentalCount.
   - if hasProfile is false: a generic account icon and a sign-in or sign-up control.
3) User clicks the sign-up or create account control in the header or main account entry area.
4) The application performs a navigation to profile.html with the URL parameter source=home_sign_up.
5) User manages profile on profile.html and may later return to index.html via header navigation or logo click.
interactions: Destination input interaction: Clicking into the destination field focuses the input; typing fires a debounced handler that calls getDestinationSuggestions(query). Selecting a suggestion sets the selectedDestination state (full Destination object) and closes the suggestion list.

Date range picker interaction: Clicking the date input opens a date range picker component; selecting a start date then an end date sets selectedCheckIn and selectedCheckOut in the form state, with validation ensuring end date is not before start date. Clearing dates resets both values.

Quick date range selection: Clicking a quick-date-range item calls a handler that computes appropriate checkIn and checkOut dates based on the option's configuration and updates the shared date state; the active quick range is highlighted until the user manually changes dates.

Guest/pet selector interaction: Clicking the guest selector opens controls for adults, children, and pets. Increment and decrement buttons adjust counts with lower bounds of zero. On confirmation or closing, counts are stored in the form state and summarized in the collapsed control.

Main search form submission: Clicking the search button or pressing Enter triggers onSubmit; client-side validation runs for destination and dates. If validation passes, startNewSearch is invoked. While waiting, the submit button is disabled and a loading state is indicated. On success, the browser is redirected to search_results.html with destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and pets encoded in the URL (along with any optional searchContextId). On error, an inline error message is displayed and no navigation occurs.

Featured destination shortcut click: Clicking a featured destination element calls a handler that sets the search form's destination selection to that destination. If the form already has valid dates and guest counts, an optional behavior may auto-focus the search button but does not auto-submit without explicit user action.
Header logo click: Clicking the site logo in the header navigates to index.html without parameters, effectively resetting the home page state to defaults (subject to fresh API calls).

Header account/profile click: Clicking the profile name or account icon opens account-related navigation (implementation outside this spec) and may navigate to profile.html (with source parameter appropriate to that action) or other pages. Clicking a sign-up action specifically navigates to profile.html?source=home_sign_up.

Wishlist indicator interaction: The wishlist icon or badge in the header reflects defaultWishlistRentalCount from getHeaderUserSummary. Clicking it may navigate to a wishlist-related page (outside current scope) without modifying home page search state.

Error handling interactions: If getHeaderUserSummary or getHomePageConfig fail, the page displays an inline, non-blocking error message and hides or disables affected components (e.g., featured destinations, quick date ranges) while still allowing the user to use the main search form with whatever defaults are available. If getDestinationSuggestions fails, the autocomplete shows a message and allows manual input but requires a valid selection to submit.
page_functionality.state_logic: The home page relies on both in-memory component state and URL query parameters. No localStorage or similar persistence is used. On load, the page parses the current URL for recognized parameters: destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets, and optionally searchContextId. If destinationId and destinationName are present and non-empty, they initialize the main search form destination state. If only one of them is present or values are invalid, the destination field starts empty. checkIn and checkOut, if provided in valid ISO 8601 format and forming a valid date range, initialize the date-range picker; otherwise they are ignored. adults, children, and pets, if present and valid non-negative integers, initialize the guest/pets selector; otherwise defaults from getHomePageConfig.defaultGuests are used. searchContextId, if present, is preserved but not used directly on the home page; it may be forwarded unchanged when navigating back to search_results.html. On any search form submission that passes validation, startNewSearch is called; once it returns a SearchContext, the page constructs a new URL for search_results.html including at minimum: destinationId=selectedDestination.id, destinationName=selectedDestination.name, checkIn=selectedCheckInISO8601, checkOut=selectedCheckOutISO8601, adults=selectedAdultsCount, children=selectedChildrenCount, pets=selectedPetsCount. Optionally, it may also include searchContextId=searchContext.id. These parameters fully define the search state for the destination page. When the user navigates back to index.html via browser back or logo click, the URL determines whether any of this state is prefilled; if no parameters are present, the page uses only getHeaderUserSummary and getHomePageConfig for initialization.

components.functionality: Initializes and renders the global header, including logo navigation to the home page, account/profile entry, and wishlist indicator based on the current user summary.
Coordinates initial API calls for the home page, including getHeaderUserSummary and getHomePageConfig, and applies URL parameter-based prefill logic for the main search form.
Holds and validates the core search parameters: selected destination, check-in/check-out dates, and guest counts (adults, children, pets). Provides the single entry point for initiating a new search.
Provides typeahead suggestions for destinations using getDestinationSuggestions and updates the main search form’s selected destination when a suggestion is chosen.
Allows the user to pick check-in and check-out dates, ensures a valid range, and synchronizes selected dates with the main search form state.
Manages the number of adults, children, and pets for the search, initializing from URL parameters if present or from getHomePageConfig.defaultGuests otherwise.
Renders quick-select options for date ranges based on getHomePageConfig.quickDateRanges and, when clicked, computes and applies a date range to the main search form.
Displays a list of featured destinations from getHomePageConfig.featuredDestinations and updates the main search form destination when a shortcut is clicked.
Performs client-side validation for the main search form (ensuring destination is selected from suggestions and dates are valid) and exposes error states/messages back to the form.
Invokes startNewSearch when the main search form is successfully submitted and handles navigation to search_results.html with appropriate URL parameters.
Uses hasProfile from getHomePageConfig and getHeaderUserSummary to decide whether to show sign-up prompts or profile access controls within the home page body (beyond the header).
Shows non-blocking error messages for failures of getHeaderUserSummary, getHomePageConfig, getDestinationSuggestions, or startNewSearch, without persisting any data locally.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; card width suggests using 3–4 columns on desktop with large outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strictly top-to-bottom, full-width header stack followed by a centered content column. At the very top, a shallow, full-width header-user-summary bar mirrors the mockup's contact strip. Directly beneath, the main header band (home-header-init) holds logo/brand context on the left and account-entry-actions on the right. Below the header, an optional home-error-banner sits as a full-width alert within the centered content container. The main content area then presents a left-aligned vertical column: first the search introduction (home-header-init's secondary content if any), then the main-search-form as a prominent card. Within that card, the destination-autocomplete, date-range-picker, guest-pets-selector, quick-date-range-selector, and search-start-controller are arranged in a left-biased grid that still feels airy. Immediately beneath the search card, still aligned to the same left column, featured-destination-shortcuts are stacked, echoing the property card stack from the mockup. The right side of the content area remains mostly empty on desktop to preserve the left-heavy, editorial feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strictly top-to-bottom, full-width header stack followed by a centered content column. At the very top, a shallow, full-width header-user-summary bar mirrors the mockup's contact strip. Directly beneath, the main header band (home-header-init) holds logo/brand context on the left and account-entry-actions on the right. Below the header, an optional home-error-banner sits as a full-width alert within the centered content container. The main content area then presents a left-aligned vertical column: first the search introduction (home-header-init's secondary content if any), then the main-search-form as a prominent card. Within that card, the destination-autocomplete, date-range-picker, guest-pets-selector, quick-date-range-selector, and search-start-controller are arranged in a left-biased grid that still feels airy. Immediately beneath the search card, still aligned to the same left column, featured-destination-shortcuts are stacked, echoing the property card stack from the mockup. The right side of the content area remains mostly empty on desktop to preserve the left-heavy, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Above-the-fold height is divided into roughly three vertical bands: ~10% for the shallow header-user-summary bar, ~15–20% for the main header (logo, navigation, account-entry-actions, and any brief intro), and the remaining viewport filled by the main content area dominated by the search card and its shortcuts. The central content container spans about 70–80% of the viewport width with generous left and right gutters. Within this container, the main-search-form card occupies roughly 35–45% of the container width on desktop and is anchored to the left, leaving the remaining 55–65% as open space on the right. Vertical gaps between major blocks (header to error banner, error banner to intro, intro to search card, search card to shortcuts) follow the 32–48px scale, while internal padding within cards and fields stays in the 16–24px range to keep the interface light and breathable.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low information density similar to the mockup: few elements on screen at once, generous white space, and clearly separated sections. Each card-like surface (search form, error banner, shortcut panels) uses 16–24px padding and ample line spacing. Form controls are grouped logically but not tightly packed; rows of inputs have 16–24px horizontal gaps and 16–24px vertical separation, avoiding a utilitarian dashboard look. Shortcut elements (quick-date-range-selector and featured-destination-shortcuts) are treated as simple, pill-like chips with loose spacing rather than dense lists, reinforcing the relaxed vacation brand.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is drawn first to the top green header-user-summary bar, then immediately down to the brand/header band where logo and account-entry-actions live, echoing the mockup's contact and navigation bands. From there, focus drops to the centered content container, where the home-error-banner (when visible) briefly interrupts flow with a high-contrast strip. Next, the eye moves to the left-aligned introduction and main-search-form card — especially the destination-autocomplete at the top-left of the card — then scans horizontally across the first row of fields, mimicking an F-pattern. The eye then moves downward through the quick-date-range-selector row and finally to the featured-destination-shortcuts, which provide a bottom anchor similar to the property cards in the mockup. Throughout, content is slightly left-weighted with wide, calm whitespace on the right.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a light, airy composition on a mostly white canvas, anchored by a strong brand green accent. At the very top, a thin green header strip spans the full width, containing compact user summary details. Directly beneath, a taller white header band holds the Hawaii vacation brand mark on the left and subtle navigation/account actions on the right, all sitting within a centered content container, with generous horizontal gutters giving the header room to breathe. As you scroll your eyes downward, the main content area appears as a clean white canvas: an optional, soft-edged error banner can appear just under the header, and below that, a left-aligned column becomes the primary focus. In this column, a relaxed introductory line and the main search card invite users to start planning their stay. The search card feels like a modern property card from the mockup: a rounded rectangle, slightly elevated with a soft shadow, taking up about 40% of the page width and hugging the left side while the right side remains largely empty. Inside, wide, pill-like fields for destination, dates, and guests are laid out in one or two rows with clear breathing room, and a prominent green search button anchors the bottom-right of the card. Below this, aligned to the same left column, compact rows of quick date range chips and featured destination shortcuts stack vertically, echoing the mockup’s stacked property cards but with a more compact, utility-focused treatment. The overall effect is a calm, vertical flow that gently guides users from top bars to search controls, with ample white space and a slight left bias that feels relaxed and premium, like a resort brochure brought into a modern web interface.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-user-summary appears as the very top band of the page, stretching full-width edge-to-edge across the viewport. It is relatively shallow, approximately 40–56px tall (about 10–12% of the total header stack height), rendered in the brand green background with white or dark text for contrast. Content inside is horizontally centered within the main content container, with 16px left and right padding from the container edges. Typical elements (like a phone number, brief user status, or support links) are aligned to the left of this container, while the right side is mostly clear or may host a very small, subtle item, keeping the strip visually dense but not cluttered. This bar anchors the top of the vertical flow and sits flush atop the home-header-init section with no gap in between, forming a cohesive header stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width bar at the top of the page that scrolls away with the rest of the content; it does not stick or float. It defines the top boundary of the layout and establishes the brand color immediately.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-header-init section sits directly beneath the header-user-summary bar, spanning the full page width but visually constrained within the centered content container. It occupies roughly 96–144px in height on desktop (about 15–20% of the initial viewport height) and uses a white background to contrast with the green strip above. Within this band, the left side (about 60% of the container width) holds the brand logo, possibly a script logo with a small palm tree icon, and a brief tagline such as “Plan your perfect Hawaii stay”, aligned left with 24px padding from the container edge. On the right side (about 40% of the container width), aligned to the top-right corner within this band, the account-entry-actions component is placed, forming a horizontal cluster. Below the logo/brand line, there may be one short line of introductory text aligned left that gently leads into the search area. The entire band sits directly above the main content area with a vertical gap of about 32px separating it from the next major block (home-error-banner or main-search-form).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static header band that scrolls away with content. It defines the top of the main content container and provides the anchor for brand and account elements, but is not sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-error-banner is positioned directly below the home-header-init band and above the main content column. It is only visible when there is a global home-level error (such as a search initialization issue). When present, it occupies the full width of the centered content container, not edge-to-edge, preserving large outer gutters. Its height is moderate (around 56–80px), depending on message length, and it appears as a rounded rectangle with a soft red-tinted background and subtle border, maintaining the card-like language of the design. It is aligned left within the container with 24px internal padding and sits on a white page background, with a 24–32px vertical gap below it before the next element (typically the main-search-form or an intro line). This placement ensures that error information is encountered naturally as users move from the header to the search controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically based on error state. It does not overlay other content; instead, it pushes the main content downward when visible, preserving the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main-search-form is the primary focal card in the main content area. It is placed below the home-header-init (and below the home-error-banner if present), aligned to the left edge of the centered content container. There is a vertical gap of approximately 32–48px from the header band or error banner above, reflecting the airy separation seen in the mockup. On desktop, the form card occupies about 35–45% of the container width, leaving a clear, empty expanse on the right to reinforce the left-weighted layout. The card itself has a white background, rounded corners, and a soft, diffuse shadow, with 24px padding on all sides. Inside, inputs are arranged in 1–2 rows: the top row places the destination-autocomplete on the far left, with the date-range-picker and guest-pets-selector sitting to its right when space allows. The search-start-controller button anchors the bottom-right of the card, either in the same row as the last field on wide screens or in a second row aligned to the right on narrower widths. Vertical spacing between rows is around 16–24px. The quick-date-range-selector may appear as a dedicated row at the bottom of the card or immediately beneath it, visually attached, with chips aligned left. The entire card sits roughly mid-screen on initial load, pulling the eye down after the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, left-anchored card that responds to viewport changes: on narrower screens it expands toward full width and its inner layout reflows from multi-column to stacked rows while maintaining generous padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the main-search-form card, the destination-autocomplete field is placed at the top-left corner of the form content area, aligning with the left padding of the card. It is the first and widest control in the top row, occupying roughly 45–55% of the card’s internal width on desktop, visually mirroring the top image emphasis in the mockup’s property cards by becoming the central starting point. The field presents as a single, rounded pill-like input with a subtle border and 12–16px internal padding, with its label either above in a compact, muted text or inside as a floating label. A small location icon may appear inside on the left for visual anchoring. To the right of this field, after a 16–24px horizontal gap, sit the other primary fields (date-range-picker and guest-pets-selector). On smaller screens, this field stretches to full width and stacks above the others, still maintaining 16–24px vertical spacing from the next element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the search form; it maintains its top-left, first-position priority across breakpoints, but its width adapts from multi-column to full-width as the card narrows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date-range-picker is positioned inside the main-search-form to the immediate right of the destination-autocomplete when horizontal space allows. It sits in the same top row, separated by a 16–24px gap, and takes up about 25–30% of the card’s internal width on desktop. The control appears as a single pill-shaped field indicating both check-in and check-out, with an inline calendar icon on the right, matching the minimal, rounded style. Its vertical alignment matches the destination field, so the tops of both inputs line up cleanly. On narrower screens where horizontal space is limited, the date-range-picker drops to a new row directly beneath the destination-autocomplete, maintaining a 16–24px vertical gap and staying left-aligned with the other fields. Below or near this control (either inside the card or immediately beneath it), the quick-date-range-selector chips are visually associated to reinforce the date selection area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and responsive; its relative position (following the destination-autocomplete) is preserved while its row/column layout shifts with the card width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guest-pets-selector sits inside the main-search-form as the last input field before the main CTA. On wide screens, it is placed to the right of the date-range-picker in the same top row, separated by another 16–24px gap, and consumes the remaining width of that row (typically about 15–25% of the card’s internal width). Its appearance matches the other fields: a rounded, pill-like control with a concise label (e.g., “Guests & Pets”) and a compact summary of the current selection. On narrower viewports, it drops beneath the date-range-picker, maintaining alignment with the left edge of the other inputs and a vertical spacing of 16–24px. The search-start-controller button is visually associated with this selector, appearing just to its right on large screens or directly below it on smaller screens. This placement reinforces the natural flow of: choose where, choose when, choose who, then search.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form, but its horizontal vs. vertical placement relative to the date-range-picker and search button adapts to available width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-date-range-selector is visually tied to the date-range-picker and is positioned either as the bottom row within the main-search-form card or immediately beneath the card, hugging its bottom-left edge. In either case, it is aligned left with the other form fields and spans around 60–80% of the card’s width, presented as a horizontal row of pill-shaped chips (e.g., “This weekend”, “Next week”, “Next month”). Each chip has generous 8–12px horizontal padding and 8px internal vertical padding, with 8–12px gaps between chips. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the main line of form inputs and this chip row. On small screens, chips wrap onto multiple lines while preserving the left alignment and spacing. This row creates a soft, editorial-looking continuation of the form, similar in visual weight to the mockup’s grid/list toggle controls above the property cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static relative to the search form; its chips wrap naturally as available width changes, but it remains visually anchored to the date-range-picker area and does not float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-destination-shortcuts component is placed directly beneath the main-search-form area, following the quick-date-range-selector in the vertical flow. It aligns with the same left edge as the search card, creating a consistent left column. There is a 32–40px vertical gap between the bottom of the search-related elements and the top of this shortcut block, echoing the separation between controls and property cards in the mockup. The component can appear as a series of horizontal rows of destination chips or compact cards (e.g., “Maui”, “Oahu”, “Kauai”), each with a rounded shape and possibly a small thumbnail or icon. The entire block occupies roughly 40–50% of the content container width on desktop, leaving the right side open, and may extend vertically as more destinations are added, stacking in 1–2 columns depending on the design choice, but always favoring a left-heavy layout. Each chip/card includes generous internal padding (16–20px) and 16–24px gaps between rows, maintaining the low-density, lifestyle feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacking vertically as more shortcuts are added. On smaller screens it can span near-full width and wrap into multiple lines while preserving comfortable spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-form-error-handler is a functional layer associated with the main-search-form rather than a visible block with its own separate area. Visually, its effects appear as inline validation messages and subtle red outlines beneath or around individual fields (destination-autocomplete, date-range-picker, guest-pets-selector) and, when necessary, as content injected into the home-error-banner above the form. Inline messages appear directly beneath the affected input, left-aligned with the field label, using small, unobtrusive text and a 4–8px vertical gap from the field. This preserves the clean card aesthetic while clearly indicating problems. The handler never introduces new containers beyond these inline labels and any content it surfaces through the existing home-error-banner; it therefore does not alter the core spatial structure of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible as its own element; it occupies no dedicated space until errors occur, at which point it slightly increases the height of the affected fields or triggers the home-error-banner, pushing subsequent elements downward in a natural flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-start-controller is the primary call-to-action button for initiating a search and is positioned within the main-search-form card. On wider screens, it is anchored to the bottom-right corner of the card’s content area, aligned with the right internal padding and the baseline of the last row of inputs (often in line with the guest-pets-selector). There is typically a 16–24px horizontal gap between the button and the nearest input field. The button is rendered as a solid brand green pill with white text, slightly larger and more prominent than the other controls, and it visually caps the form, similar to how a price tag anchors the bottom of a property card. On narrower screens, the button reflows to sit on its own row directly beneath the last input field, centered or right-aligned but still within the card’s width, with a 16–24px vertical gap above it. The height of the button is generous (around 40–48px) to give a touch-friendly and inviting feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form; it maintains a bottom-right emphasis while adapting from inline placement to its own row as the card layout stacks on smaller devices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-entry-actions component lives within the home-header-init band, aligned to the far right of the centered content container. It forms a compact horizontal cluster of elements (e.g., “Sign in”, “My trips”, a heart icon for wishlist) set against the white header background. Vertically, it is aligned to the center of the header band height so that its baseline roughly matches that of the logo text on the left, creating a clear left-right balance. The action items themselves are spaced with 16–24px gaps, using light typography and small icons to maintain an unobtrusive presence. There is at least 24px padding between the rightmost action and the container edge. This placement mirrors the mockup’s navigation menu occupying the top-right region and keeps the focus on the main content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, attached to the right side of the main header band. It does not stick as users scroll; once scrolled past the header, these controls leave the viewport with the rest of the header.

name: Search Results
title: Search Results
description: Displays Hawaii vacation rental search results for a selected destination, dates, and guests, with filters, sorting, and list/map views based on the active SearchContext.

core_features: Initialize and maintain an active SearchContext using URL parameters together with getActiveSearchContext and startNewSearch.
Render a list of rental properties for the active SearchContext using getSearchResultsList, showing key rental details and enabling navigation to listing details.
Provide filter controls (property type, bedrooms, price, rating, amenities, cancellation, long-stay discount) sourced from getSearchFilterOptions and applied via updateSearchFiltersAndSort.
Provide sort options (e.g., price and rating) sourced from getSearchFilterOptions.sortOptions and applied via updateSearchFiltersAndSort.
Allow users to toggle between list and map views using setSearchViewMode, persisting view mode in SearchContext and URL parameters.
Render a map view of rentals within visible bounds using getSearchResultsMapData, with interactive markers that connect to listing.html.
Allow saving rentals to a wishlist from the results list or map markers using saveRentalToWishlist, leveraging default wishlist behavior from getHeaderUserSummary.
Support starting a new search (destination, dates, guests, pets) from the header or summary bar, updating URL parameters and SearchContext via startNewSearch.
Offer destination auto-suggestions in the header search using getDestinationSuggestions while typing.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Landing on Search Results from another page: (1) User submits a search on index.html or another page; the browser navigates to search_results.html with destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets, and optional filter/sort/viewMode URL parameters. (2) On load, the page parses URL parameters. (3) The page calls getActiveSearchContext. If no matching active search exists for the URL parameters, the page calls startNewSearch with the parsed destinationId, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets. (4) The returned searchContext is set as active; initial results are taken from either startNewSearch.results or, if the context already exists, from getSearchResultsList. (5) If filter/sort parameters are present in the URL, the page calls updateSearchFiltersAndSort to apply them and refresh results. (6) If viewMode is present in the URL, the page calls setSearchViewMode to align SearchContext.view_mode to the URL value.

Workflow 2 – Applying and adjusting filters/sort in list view: (1) User sees filter controls populated using getSearchFilterOptions and current values from SearchContext. (2) User adjusts one or more filter controls (e.g., minBedrooms, maxPricePerNight, amenities, cancellationFreeMinDaysBefore, requireLongStayDiscount, propertyType, minRating). (3) On any change, the page constructs the full filter and sort payload from the current UI state and calls updateSearchFiltersAndSort. (4) The updated searchContext and results are returned; the list view is re-rendered with the new RentalProperty array. (5) The browser URL query string is updated to reflect the new filter and sort parameters so the state is shareable and restorable. (6) If the user updates only the sort option, the same updateSearchFiltersAndSort call is used with unchanged filters and a new sortOption.

Workflow 3 – Switching between list and map views: (1) User toggles view mode via a dedicated control. (2) The page calls setSearchViewMode with 'list' or 'map'. (3) The returned searchContext.view_mode is used to conditionally show either the list or the map component and to update the viewMode URL parameter. (4) When switching to map view, the map component calculates initial mapBounds based on the destination and current zoom, then calls getSearchResultsMapData(mapBounds) to fetch rentals for markers. (5) When panning or zooming the map, the map component repeatedly calls getSearchResultsMapData with the new mapBounds to refresh markers.

Workflow 4 – Viewing rental details from list or map: (1) In list view, user clicks anywhere on a rental result card (excluding wishlist controls). (2) The page reads the selected RentalProperty.id and current active SearchContext.check_in, check_out, adults_count, children_count, pets_count. (3) The browser navigates to listing.html with rentalId and the guest/date parameters encoded in the URL query. (4) In map view, user clicks a marker representing a rental. (5) The marker click either opens a small preview card or directly triggers navigation; when user chooses to view full details, the same navigation to listing.html occurs with the same parameter structure.

Workflow 5 – Saving rentals to wishlist from results: (1) User clicks a wishlist/save icon on a rental card in list view or on a marker preview in map view. (2) The handler calls saveRentalToWishlist with the rentalPropertyId (and optionally a wishlistId if the UI allows choosing one; otherwise omitted to use the default wishlist defined in the backend). (3) On success, the component updates its local visual state (e.g., toggling the saved icon) based on the response, and may re-render any count indicators that derive from wishlist or HeaderUserSummary data. (4) If needed, the header can optionally re-call getHeaderUserSummary to refresh the defaultWishlistRentalCount shown in the header.

Workflow 6 – Starting a new search from the header: (1) User edits the destination, dates, and guests in the global header search. Destination suggestions are fetched as they type using getDestinationSuggestions. (2) On submit, the header computes destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets. (3) The browser navigates to search_results.html with those values as URL parameters (replacing the current query). (4) The page load logic runs again: parse URL, call getActiveSearchContext, potentially call startNewSearch, and render results accordingly. (5) Any filters and sort parameters not specified in the new URL are reset to defaults in the new SearchContext.
interactions: Interaction – Initial page load: onPageLoad -> parse URL parameters -> call getActiveSearchContext -> if hasActiveSearch is false or its key fields differ from URL (destination_id, check_in, check_out, adults_count, children_count, pets_count), call startNewSearch with parsed values -> set active SearchContext from result. If filter or sort URL parameters are present, call updateSearchFiltersAndSort with those values before rendering results. If viewMode URL parameter is set, call setSearchViewMode to sync SearchContext.view_mode.

Interaction – Filter change: onFilterChange (any control in filter bar) -> collect full set of filter/sort values from UI state or SearchContext -> call updateSearchFiltersAndSort(propertyType, minBedrooms, maxPricePerNight, minRating, amenities, cancellationFreeMinDaysBefore, requireLongStayDiscount, sortOption) -> update in-memory SearchContext and results list from response -> update URL parameters to match new filter/sort state.

Interaction – Sort change: onSortChange (sort dropdown) -> call updateSearchFiltersAndSort with existing filters and updated sortOption -> re-render list view with returned results -> update sortOption in URL.

Interaction – View mode toggle: onViewModeToggle('list'|'map') -> call setSearchViewMode(viewMode) -> update SearchContext.view_mode -> update viewMode URL parameter -> conditionally display list or map component. If switching to 'map', initialize map if needed and call getSearchResultsMapData(currentMapBounds).

Interaction – Map movement: onMapIdle (after pan or zoom) -> read current mapBounds from the map component -> call getSearchResultsMapData(mapBounds) -> update marker set in the map view with returned RentalProperty data.

Interaction – Click rental card: onResultCardClick(rentalId) -> read active SearchContext.check_in, check_out, adults_count, children_count, pets_count -> navigate to listing.html with URL query { rentalId, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets }.

Interaction – Click map marker: onMapMarkerClick(rentalId) -> optionally highlight associated list card -> onMarkerViewDetails -> navigate to listing.html with same parameter structure as list card navigation.

Interaction – Save to wishlist (list view): onWishlistIconClick(rentalId) -> call saveRentalToWishlist(rentalPropertyId = rentalId, wishlistId = optionalSelectedWishlistId) -> if response.success is true, update local saved state for that rental and optionally refresh header summary via getHeaderUserSummary.
Interaction – Save to wishlist (map view): onMapMarkerWishlistClick(rentalId) -> same as list view save interaction using saveRentalToWishlist.
Interaction – Header search typing: onDestinationInputChange(queryText) -> call getDestinationSuggestions(query = queryText) -> display updated suggestions list for user to select a destination.

Interaction – Header search submit: onHeaderSearchSubmit(formValues) -> construct URL query with destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets (and optionally clear filters/sort) -> navigate to search_results.html with new query string, triggering full page reload and new SearchContext initialization.

Interaction – Quick date/guest change in summary bar (if exposed): onSummaryBarChange(newDatesOrGuests) -> call startNewSearch with updated dates/guest counts and current destinationId -> set SearchContext and replace URL with updated parameters -> refresh results from startNewSearch.results or via subsequent updateSearchFiltersAndSort if filters are still applied.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary source of shareable state is the URL query string. On each page load, the application reads destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets, propertyType, minBedrooms, maxPricePerNight, minRating, amenities, cancellationFreeMinDaysBefore, requireLongStayDiscount, sortOption, and viewMode from the URL. These values are treated as the desired state and are reconciled with the active SearchContext provided by getActiveSearchContext. If getActiveSearchContext.hasActiveSearch is false, or if its core search parameters (destination_id, check_in, check_out, adults_count, children_count, pets_count) do not match the corresponding URL parameters, the page calls startNewSearch using the URL values, thus creating a new SearchContext that will be used for subsequent operations. If filter and sort parameters exist in the URL, updateSearchFiltersAndSort is called with those values to ensure the SearchContext and results align with the URL. The SearchContext fields property_type_filter, min_bedrooms, max_price_per_night, min_rating, amenities_filter, cancellation_free_min_days_before, require_long_stay_discount, sort_option, and view_mode represent the authoritative in-memory state. Whenever the user changes a filter or sort option, updateSearchFiltersAndSort is called with the new values and the response's SearchContext is considered canonical. After each such update, the URL query string is rewritten to mirror the SearchContext fields (with amenities_filter serialized as a comma-separated list for the amenities URL parameter). When the user toggles view mode, setSearchViewMode(viewMode) is called, which updates SearchContext.view_mode; the viewMode URL parameter is then updated to the same value so that reloading or sharing the URL preserves the chosen view. All navigations to listing.html include rentalId and the current SearchContext dates and guest counts as URL parameters, ensuring that the listing page can compute pricing and availability for the same stay configuration. No client-side persistence such as localStorage is used; reloading the page always reconstructs state from the URL parameters plus the server-side SearchContext accessed via getActiveSearchContext and the search interfaces.

components.functionality: Displays user summary (profile name and default wishlist status), provides global search inputs for destination, dates, guests, and pets, and sends the user to an updated Search Results state when a new search is submitted.
event_handlers: onHeaderLoad -> getHeaderUserSummary() to populate profile and wishlist indicators.
onDestinationInputChange(query) -> getDestinationSuggestions(query) to display destination suggestions.

onHeaderSearchSubmit(formValues) -> build URL with destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets -> navigate to search_results.html with updated query parameters (page reload handles startNewSearch / getActiveSearchContext).

components.functionality: Central controller that initializes and synchronizes the active SearchContext with URL parameters on page load and after navigation.
event_handlers: onPageLoad -> parse URL parameters -> getActiveSearchContext() -> if no matching active search, call startNewSearch(destinationId, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets) -> set SearchContext from response.

onPageLoadAfterContext -> if filter/sort params exist (propertyType, minBedrooms, maxPricePerNight, minRating, amenities, cancellationFreeMinDaysBefore, requireLongStayDiscount, sortOption), call updateSearchFiltersAndSort(...) with those values to align SearchContext and results to the URL.
onPageLoadAfterFilters -> if viewMode parameter exists, call setSearchViewMode(viewMode) to ensure SearchContext.view_mode matches URL.
onSearchContextChange -> update URL query parameters to reflect the current SearchContext fields used on this page.

components.functionality: Shows a concise summary of the current search (destination, dates, guest counts, pets) and may allow quick adjustments that trigger a new search.
event_handlers: onSummaryRender -> read SearchContext.destination_name, check_in, check_out, adults_count, children_count, pets_count to display search summary.

onSummaryEditSubmit(updatedDestinationAndDates) -> optionally call startNewSearch(destinationId, checkIn, checkOut, adultsCount, childrenCount, petsCount) -> update SearchContext and replace URL parameters with new core search values.

components.functionality: Displays and manages property filters such as property type, bedrooms, price, rating, amenities, cancellation policy, and long-stay discount, sourced dynamically and applied to update results.
event_handlers: onFilterBarLoad -> getSearchFilterOptions() to obtain propertyTypes, bedroomOptions, ratingOptions, amenitiesOptions, cancellationPolicyOptions, sortOptions for rendering controls.

onFilterChange(propertyType, minBedrooms, maxPricePerNight, minRating, amenities, cancellationFreeMinDaysBefore, requireLongStayDiscount) -> merge with current sortOption from SearchContext -> call updateSearchFiltersAndSort(...) -> update SearchContext and results list with response -> update URL query parameters accordingly.

components.functionality: Provides sort selection (e.g., by price or rating) and applies the chosen option to the active SearchContext and results.
event_handlers: onSortControlLoad -> getSearchFilterOptions() to read sortOptions and populate dropdown.

onSortChange(sortOption) -> read current filter values from SearchContext or filter-bar state -> call updateSearchFiltersAndSort(propertyType, minBedrooms, maxPricePerNight, minRating, amenities, cancellationFreeMinDaysBefore, requireLongStayDiscount, sortOption) -> update SearchContext and results -> write sortOption to URL.

components.functionality: Allows users to switch between list view and map view while keeping the chosen mode in sync with SearchContext and the URL.
event_handlers: onToggleToList -> setSearchViewMode('list') -> update SearchContext.view_mode -> update viewMode URL parameter -> show results-list component and hide map-view.

onToggleToMap -> setSearchViewMode('map') -> update SearchContext.view_mode -> update viewMode URL parameter -> show map-view component and hide results-list -> trigger initial map data loading if needed.

components.functionality: Displays the current list of RentalProperty items for the active SearchContext, with key details, wishlist actions, and navigation to the listing page.
event_handlers: onResultsListLoad -> if initial results were returned by startNewSearch or updateSearchFiltersAndSort, use them; otherwise call getSearchResultsList() to fetch RentalProperty array.

onResultCardClick(rentalId) -> read SearchContext.check_in, check_out, adults_count, children_count, pets_count -> navigate to listing.html?rentalId=...&checkIn=...&checkOut=...&adults=...&children=...&pets=....

onWishlistIconClick(rentalId, optionalWishlistId) -> call saveRentalToWishlist(rentalPropertyId = rentalId, wishlistId = optionalWishlistId) -> if success, mark the rental as saved in local component state and optionally refresh header via getHeaderUserSummary.

components.functionality: Shows rentals as markers on an interactive map constrained to the current SearchContext, loading data only for the visible map bounds and enabling navigation and wishlist actions from markers.
event_handlers: onMapInit -> determine initial mapBounds based on SearchContext.destination_id and destination configuration -> call getSearchResultsMapData(mapBounds) to retrieve RentalProperty markers.
onMapIdle(newMapBounds) -> call getSearchResultsMapData(newMapBounds) to refresh markers as the user pans or zooms.
onMapMarkerClick(rentalId) -> optionally highlight corresponding item if list is visible -> show marker preview.
onMarkerViewDetails(rentalId) -> read SearchContext.check_in, check_out, adults_count, children_count, pets_count -> navigate to listing.html with those parameters.
onMarkerWishlistClick(rentalId, optionalWishlistId) -> call saveRentalToWishlist(rentalPropertyId = rentalId, wishlistId = optionalWishlistId) -> update marker saved state on success.

components.functionality: Handles the case where the current filters return no RentalProperty results, offering options to adjust filters or clear them.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdated(resultsArray) -> if array is empty, display empty-state component; otherwise hide it.

onClearFiltersClick -> call updateSearchFiltersAndSort with minimal or default filter values and current sortOption -> update SearchContext and results -> update URL parameters to reflect cleared filters.

components.functionality: Provides feedback when rentals are added to a wishlist, using the response from saveRentalToWishlist and optionally updating header indicators.
event_handlers: onWishlistSaveResponse(response) -> if response.success is true, display confirmation based on response.message and wishlist data.
onWishlistStateChange -> optionally call getHeaderUserSummary() to reflect updated defaultWishlistRentalCount in the header.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; card width suggests using 3–4 columns on desktop with large outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strictly vertical, top-to-bottom arrangement: a full-width two-tier header (contact strip + main nav), followed by a centered content container. Inside the container, place the search summary first, then a horizontal filter bar, then a compact controls row (view toggle on the left, sort on the right), and finally the main results region, which alternates between a vertical list of cards or a full-width map. Non-visual controllers (search-context-initializer, wishlist-feedback logic) sit logically at the top of the DOM and overlay when needed but do not disrupt the core linear flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strictly vertical, top-to-bottom arrangement: a full-width two-tier header (contact strip + main nav), followed by a centered content container. Inside the container, place the search summary first, then a horizontal filter bar, then a compact controls row (view toggle on the left, sort on the right), and finally the main results region, which alternates between a vertical list of cards or a full-width map. Non-visual controllers (search-context-initializer, wishlist-feedback logic) sit logically at the top of the DOM and overlay when needed but do not disrupt the core linear flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate the very top ~15–20% of the initial viewport height to the header-bar (with roughly 1/3 of that for the shallow contact strip and 2/3 for the main nav). The remaining 80–85% is reserved for the search results experience. Within the content container (max-width ~1200–1280px, centered), devote the upper band to summary + filters + controls (stacked with 24–32px gaps), and the rest to the results-list or map-view. In list mode, the cards column occupies roughly 40–50% of the content width on the left, leaving open white space on the right to preserve the airy feel; in map mode, the map expands to use nearly the full content width.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low density and a relaxed, editorial feel: generous horizontal gutters on both sides of the content container, 32–48px vertical spacing between major sections, and 16–24px internal padding in cards and bars. Show only one primary interaction band at a time (filters row, then a slim controls row, then the cards or map), avoiding crowded toolbars or multi-row controls. The filters are expressed as a few pill-shaped buttons rather than dense form fields, and the results cards use large photography and minimal text.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye in a top-to-bottom, left-biased F-pattern. The high-contrast green contact strip at the very top grabs attention, then the logo and navigation directly below. From there, the eye moves down to the search summary, then to the filter bar, then to the left-aligned view-mode toggle. The vertical stack of large, image-first property cards or, alternatively, the map_view then becomes the dominant area of focus. Controls (filters, view toggle, sort) remain aligned to the left and right of a single horizontal band so they are seen early but don’t compete with the imagery-heavy results content.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a full-width header that has a slim brand-green contact strip on top and a taller white navigation band below, stretching edge-to-edge across the viewport. Beneath this, a centered content container sits on a white canvas with wide left and right gutters. At the top of this container, a subtle, card-like search summary bar runs horizontally, left-aligned and breathing with generous vertical padding. Just below, a light, understated filter bar spans the same width, with pill-shaped filter controls clustered toward the left. A short distance beneath that, a slim controls row holds the view-mode toggle anchored to the left and the sort dropdown anchored to the right. Below this control area, the main results region unfolds: in list mode, a single vertical column of large, image-led property cards occupies roughly the left half of the container, leaving the right half mostly open, reinforcing the airy, editorial feeling; in map mode, a large map takes over the content width, with the controls hovering just above it. Throughout, whitespace is generous, cards are softly shadowed with rounded corners, and the brand green is used sparingly to highlight interactive elements and prices. If there are no results, a centered empty state card appears where the list would be, surrounded by clean white space. Any wishlist feedback appears as a light, unobtrusive toast near the top-right of the content area, floating above but not disturbing the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-bar spans the full width of the viewport at the very top of the page, occupying roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height. It is composed of two horizontal layers: a shallow contact strip on top and a taller navigation band below. The contact strip is a slim bar in brand green, about 40px tall on desktop, running edge-to-edge. Its content (e.g., phone number, quick links) is aligned within the same max-width centered container as the rest of the page, with text slightly left-biased and compactly laid out to keep it feeling dense but not cluttered. Directly beneath this strip, the main navigation band is a white bar about 80–96px tall. Within the centered content width, the resort-style logo (with script and palm icon) sits on the far left, and horizontal navigation links align to the right. The nav bar casts a very soft shadow or subtle bottom border to separate it from the content below. There is approximately 32px of whitespace between the bottom of this header and the top of the search summary bar, preserving the airy vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow (not sticky by default), full-width. Internal content constrained to a centered max-width container aligned with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-context-initializer operates as a non-visual controller and does not render any visible UI. Logically, it sits near the top of the page structure, just after the header-bar in the DOM, ensuring that the search state is established before the visible search-summary-bar, filters, and results render. It occupies no physical space on the page and does not affect the visual layout; its presence is purely functional, wiring up the active destination, dates, guests, filters, and view mode that other components display.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered / no visual footprint; participates in data flow only.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-summary-bar appears immediately below the header-bar, inside the centered content container. It stretches horizontally across roughly 80–90% of the viewport width, capped by the global max-width (e.g., 1200–1280px) with generous left and right margins. Visually, it reads as a low, subtle card or bar about 56–72px tall, with 16–24px of vertical padding and 24–32px of horizontal padding inside. The summary text (destination, dates, guests) is left-aligned and sits near the upper-left of the content area, starting in line with where the results cards will later appear, reinforcing the left-biased visual flow. On the right side of this bar, there is room for a secondary action such as a 'Modify search' link or button, aligned to the right edge of the same container. There is a clear vertical margin of about 24–32px below this bar before the filter-bar begins, keeping sections distinctly separated.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow, full-width within the main content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-bar is positioned directly below the search-summary-bar, maintaining the same centered max-width and left/right margins. It reads as a light, horizontal control strip approximately 48–64px tall, with 16px of vertical padding and 24–32px of horizontal padding. The main filter trigger (e.g., 'Filters' with an icon and count) is placed at the far left, aligned exactly with the left edge of the search-summary text and the upcoming cards. Additional quick filters (price, property type, amenities) appear as pill-shaped buttons that flow horizontally to the right and wrap to a second line on smaller screens. The overall effect is a loose row of pills hugging the left side, with ample whitespace to the right to preserve the airy layout. There is a distinct vertical gap of around 16–24px between the bottom of the filter-bar and the top of the controls row that contains the view-mode-toggle and sort-control.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, spanning the full content width inside the centered container. Pills wrap responsively but the bar itself remains a single horizontal band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control appears within a slim horizontal controls row positioned just below the filter-bar. This controls row is about 40–48px tall and aligns within the same centered content container. The sort dropdown is anchored to the far right of this row, visually balancing the view-mode-toggle on the left. The dropdown itself is a compact, minimal field (e.g., label 'Sort by' with a down-chevron) that sits about 24–32px from the right edge of the content container. Its vertical alignment matches the centerline of the view-mode-toggle, creating a clean, single-line toolbar. There is a 16–24px vertical gap below this controls row before the first result card (or map) begins, reinforcing separation between controls and content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned within a horizontal controls row that shares its space with the view-mode-toggle.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view-mode-toggle lives in the same slim controls row as the sort-control, positioned directly below the filter-bar. It is anchored to the far left of this row, aligned precisely with the left edge of the filter pills and the upcoming results cards. Visually it appears as a small, pill-shaped segmented control (e.g., 'List' and 'Map' options, possibly with icons) about 120–160px wide and 32–40px tall. The active mode is highlighted with brand green, while the inactive mode sits in a subtle, low-contrast state. Its small footprint and tight alignment near the left margin mirror the mockup's pattern of compact grid/list toggles preceding the content. When the user switches modes, the content area below either displays the vertical results-list (List view) or swaps to a large map-view (Map view), but the toggle itself remains fixed in this position at the top of the main content region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, left-aligned in the controls row. Tapping it triggers a mode change that conditionally shows either results-list or map-view below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-list occupies the main content region beneath the controls row when the view-mode-toggle is in List mode. The list is anchored to the left side of the centered content container, forming a vertical column of property cards. On desktop, this column is relatively narrow, taking up approximately 40–50% of the content container’s width (about 4–5 out of 12 grid columns), with the remaining 50–60% to the right left mostly as open whitespace. Each card is full-width within this column, with a large, full-bleed property photo at the top (spanning the card’s width), followed by a text block below that includes title, location, and price. Cards use 16–24px padding, soft shadows, and rounded corners, and they are separated vertically by 24–32px of whitespace to preserve the editorial feel. The top edge of the first card sits roughly 16–24px below the controls row. As the user scrolls, cards stack vertically down the page, maintaining the left-biased column, while the right side remains open, emphasizing the relaxed, airy layout and allowing the imagery to feel prominent and uncrowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow, visible only in List mode. In Map mode, this component is hidden or minimized so it does not consume vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The map-view shares the same vertical slot as the results-list, appearing directly beneath the controls row when the view-mode-toggle is in Map mode. Instead of a narrow left column, the map spans nearly the entire width of the centered content container, leaving only standard outer gutters (approximately 5–10% of the viewport on each side) to maintain the airy framing. The map’s height on desktop is substantial—roughly 50–60% of the viewport height—so it feels immersive without overwhelming the page. The top of the map sits 16–24px below the controls row. Key map UI (pins, zoom controls, legend) stays light and minimal so the overall aesthetic remains clean. When the user scrolls, the map moves with the page like any other block of content (no full-screen takeover by default), and the white page background continues below for any additional supporting content or pagination indicators. In List mode, this map block is completely hidden so it does not affect layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow but conditionally rendered—visible only when Map mode is active. It occupies the full content width beneath the controls row, replacing the results-list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no-results-state appears in the main content region exactly where the results-list or map-view would normally be, but only when there are no matching properties. It is positioned beneath the controls row with a 24–32px vertical gap, centered horizontally within the content container. The empty-state card is moderately sized—about 40–50% of the container width—so it feels substantial without dominating the screen, and it is vertically padded (24–32px) with a simple illustration or icon at the top, a clear heading, and a short explanatory message. The card uses soft shadows and rounded corners consistent with other cards. Surrounding it is ample whitespace on all sides, especially to the right and below, giving the impression of a calm, uncluttered space. Controls like a 'Clear filters' or 'Adjust search' button are placed near the bottom of this card, aligned center. Both results-list and map-view are hidden when this empty state is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, conditionally rendered in place of results-list or map-view. Centered within the content container with generous surrounding whitespace.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-feedback component surfaces as a small, transient toast notification that overlays the main content without reflowing it. Visually, it appears near the top-right corner of the content container, roughly aligned vertically with the lower edge of the header-bar or just above the search-summary-bar. The toast is a compact, rounded rectangle with 12–16px internal padding, a soft shadow, and a background that incorporates the brand green (or a soft green tint) to indicate success. It sits about 24px inset from the right edge of the container and 24–32px below the bottom of the header, floating above the white canvas. When triggered (e.g., when a user adds a property to their wishlist from a card), it fades in over the existing layout, remains visible for a short duration, then fades out, all without affecting the position of other components. On smaller screens, it can shift slightly inward but always remains in the upper-right quadrant of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay / toast behavior: positioned relative to the viewport or main content container, not part of normal document flow, appearing and disappearing transiently.

name: Listing Details
title: Listing Details
description: Displays full details for a single vacation rental and lets the user set stay dates/guests, see a live price quote, save the rental to a wishlist, start a booking, or message the host.

core_features: Load and display full rental information for the rentalId URL parameter using getRentalDetails, including title, description, capacity, amenities, photos, and location.
Determine the current stay context (dates and guest counts) from URL parameters and/or getActiveSearchContext, and use it to prefill the date/guest controls.
Retrieve and display a stay-specific price quote for the selected dates and guests using getStayPriceQuote, including nights, nightly rate, fees, discounts, and total before taxes.
Show a clear, interactive price and fee breakdown panel that updates whenever the stay context changes via getStayPriceQuote.
Provide a primary Book now / Reserve action that uses startBookingFromCurrentSearch with the current active search context, then navigates to checkout.html with the returned bookingId.
Allow users to quickly save the rental to a wishlist using saveRentalToWishlist (default wishlist when no wishlistId is provided).
Allow users to create a new wishlist with createWishlist and then save the current rental into that wishlist via saveRentalToWishlist.
Offer an inline or modal host messaging form that sends questions about the rental using sendHostMessageForRental.
Render a global header search that uses getDestinationSuggestions for destination autocomplete and startNewSearch to create a new search context before navigating to search_results.html.
Show approximate location and distance-to-key-area data (such as distance_to_waikiki_beach_miles) from RentalProperty on a map/location section.
user_workflows: Open listing details from search results: User clicks a rental in search_results.html -> browser navigates to listing.html with rentalId and optional checkIn/checkOut/adults/children/pets URL parameters -> page calls getHeaderUserSummary and getActiveSearchContext -> page calls getRentalDetails(rentalId) -> page derives initial stay dates/guests from URL params or active search context -> if dates and guests are available, page calls getStayPriceQuote and shows the price breakdown.

Open listing from wishlist: User clicks a saved rental in wishlist.html -> browser navigates to listing.html with rentalId (no dates/guests) -> page calls getHeaderUserSummary, getActiveSearchContext, and getRentalDetails -> if active search context exists and is compatible, its dates/guests are used as initial stay; otherwise the stay picker is empty and no price quote is shown until the user selects dates/guests.

Adjust dates/guests and update price: User edits check-in/check-out or guests in the stay-context controller -> component updates local stay state -> page calls startNewSearch with rental.destination_id and the new dates/guest counts to update the active SearchContext (no navigation) -> page calls getStayPriceQuote with rentalId and the same dates/guest counts -> price quote panel refreshes to show updated nights, rates, fees, discounts, and total.

Start booking from listing: User confirms desired dates/guests in the stay context -> user clicks the Book now / Reserve button -> page ensures there is an active SearchContext matching the current stay state (calling startNewSearch first if necessary using the current dates/guests and rental.destination_id) -> page calls startBookingFromCurrentSearch(rentalId) -> on success, page reads booking.id from the response and navigates to checkout.html?bookingId=booking.id.

Save rental to default wishlist: User clicks the wishlist (save) button when not in an expanded wishlist dialog -> page calls saveRentalToWishlist with rentalPropertyId and no wishlistId -> on success, the component shows confirmation, updates any displayed default wishlist count based on the returned Wishlist, and optionally reveals a link to wishlists.html.

Save rental to a new wishlist: User opens the wishlist dialog from the listing (e.g., via a 'Save to list' control) -> user enters a wishlist name and optional description -> on submit, page calls createWishlist(name, description, makeDefault flag as chosen by the user) -> on success, page calls saveRentalToWishlist with rentalPropertyId and the new wishlist.id -> success message is shown and a 'View all wishlists' link can be used to navigate to wishlists.html.

Message the host from listing: If host_contact_allowed is true for the rental, user opens the message host form -> user types a question and submits -> page calls sendHostMessageForRental with rentalPropertyId and messageText, leaving relatedBookingId empty or null -> on success, the form clears and a confirmation state is displayed.

Start a new search from the global header: User focuses the destination field in the header -> as user types, header calls getDestinationSuggestions(query) and shows the suggestions -> user selects a destination and enters dates/guests -> on search submit, page calls startNewSearch with destinationId, checkIn, checkOut, adultsCount, childrenCount, petsCount -> page reads the returned searchContext and navigates to search_results.html with URL params derived from the submitted destination and dates/guests.

Navigate from listing to search results for current dates: Optional pattern: user clicks a control such as 'See more places for these dates' in the booking panel -> page uses the current stay state (destinationId from rental.destination_id and dates/guests from the stay controller) to build a search_results.html URL with destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets -> browser navigates directly using URL parameters without additional APIs.
interactions: Click on rental card (on previous page) -> navigates to listing.html?rentalId=... with optional stay parameters.
On page load -> triggers fetch of header summary via getHeaderUserSummary, active search via getActiveSearchContext, and rental data via getRentalDetails using the rentalId URL parameter.
Change check-in or check-out date in the stay context component -> updates local stay state, calls startNewSearch to sync the SearchContext, and then calls getStayPriceQuote for the current rental.
Change adults/children/pets counts in the stay context component -> updates local stay state and triggers the same flow as date change (startNewSearch then getStayPriceQuote).

Click Book now / Reserve button -> validates that dates/guests are set, ensures active SearchContext is aligned (using startNewSearch when needed), then calls startBookingFromCurrentSearch and navigates to checkout.html with bookingId on success.
Click wishlist (save) icon/button -> calls saveRentalToWishlist with rentalPropertyId and optionally a selected wishlistId; updates UI with saved state and offers navigation to wishlists.html.
Open wishlist dialog -> allows user to enter a new wishlist name/description and optionally mark it as default; on submit calls createWishlist and then saveRentalToWishlist.
Submit host message form -> calls sendHostMessageForRental with rentalPropertyId and message text; shows success or error feedback.
Type into header destination input -> calls getDestinationSuggestions with the entered text and shows suggestion list; selecting a suggestion fills the destination field.
Submit header search form -> calls startNewSearch, then navigates to search_results.html with destination and stay details as URL parameters.
Click link or control to see all wishlists after saving -> navigates to wishlists.html (no additional parameters).
Interact with map/location component (e.g., open map, view distance) -> reads coordinates and distance fields from RentalProperty to render location data (no additional API calls).
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary source of state is the URL query string combined with API responses. On initial load, the page parses rentalId (required) and optional checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets from the URL. It then calls getHeaderUserSummary and getActiveSearchContext to obtain header and search state and getRentalDetails(rentalId) to obtain the RentalProperty. Initial stay state is resolved as follows: if URL provides checkIn and checkOut and guest counts, those values are used; otherwise, if getActiveSearchContext.hasActiveSearch is true, the page uses searchContext.check_in, searchContext.check_out, and guest counts; if neither source is available, the stay state is empty and no quote is loaded until the user edits the stay controls. Whenever stay state (dates/guests) changes via user interaction, the page updates internal UI state, calls startNewSearch with rental.destination_id and the updated stay parameters to keep the global SearchContext in sync (without changing the current URL), and then calls getStayPriceQuote with the same parameters. The URL is not rewritten when the user changes dates inside listing.html unless the user explicitly triggers navigation (e.g., clicking a control that leads to search_results.html or submitting the header search). Booking relies on the current active SearchContext: when Book now / Reserve is clicked, if the internal stay state differs from the active search context, the page first calls startNewSearch using the current stay state so that startBookingFromCurrentSearch uses the correct dates and guests; after that, startBookingFromCurrentSearch(rentalId) is called, and on success, the page navigates to checkout.html?bookingId={booking.id}. Wishlist state is not persisted via URL; it is derived from getHeaderUserSummary (for the default wishlist) and from the return values of saveRentalToWishlist and createWishlist for interactions on this page. Host messages are one-off actions whose state is local to the page and not reflected in the URL. Navigation to other pages always passes state via URL parameters as described in outgoing_connections.
