components.functionality: Provides a global destination/date/guest search bar at the top of the page, prefilled from getActiveSearchContext when available, and allows starting a new search that navigates to search_results.html.
Displays basic user/account information and default wishlist summary in the header based on getHeaderUserSummary, such as profileName and defaultWishlistRentalCount.

Shows the main rental information for the selected rentalId, including title, property_type, capacity, rating, review_count, description, amenities overview, and location label, all driven by getRentalDetails.
Displays the rental photos and primary_photo from the RentalProperty, allowing users to browse images; this component is purely driven by the RentalProperty.photos array and does not call APIs.

Allows the user to view and edit the stay parameters (check-in, check-out, adults, children, pets). Initializes from URL parameters or SearchContext, and when changed, triggers updates to the active search context and the stay price quote.

Shows current stay price details (nights, nightly rate, cleaning fee, service fee, other fees, long-stay discount, promo discount if any, and total before taxes) based on getStayPriceQuote. It updates whenever stay-context-controller reports a valid set of dates and guests.

Provides the primary Book now / Reserve button and related messages (e.g., instant booking availability). When clicked, ensures there is an up-to-date SearchContext for the current stay and then calls startBookingFromCurrentSearch before navigating to checkout.html with bookingId in the URL.

Renders a save/unsave control for the current rental. When activated, it saves the rental to a wishlist using saveRentalToWishlist (default wishlist by omitting wishlistId) or, if invoked from the wishlist dialog, with a specific wishlistId. Shows success, error, and an optional link to wishlists.html.

Allows the user to create a new wishlist and save the current rental to it. It gathers a wishlist name and optional description, calls createWishlist, then calls saveRentalToWishlist with the new wishlist.id and the current rentalPropertyId.

Provides a text input area for the guest to send a message to the host for the current rental when host_contact_allowed is true. On submit, calls sendHostMessageForRental with rentalPropertyId and the composed message, then displays a confirmation or error.

Displays an interactive or embedded map and textual location information using RentalProperty.latitude, RentalProperty.longitude, location_label, and distance_to_waikiki_beach_miles. No additional APIs are called here; all data comes from getRentalDetails.

Shows cancellation policy details using either the RentalProperty fields (cancellation_policy_type, supports_free_cancellation, free_cancellation_min_days_before_checkin) and/or the policy-related fields from getStayPriceQuote when a quote is available. It updates when a new quote arrives.
Works with header-global-search to display and select destination suggestions returned by getDestinationSuggestions as the user types in the destination field.

Centralizes success and error messages for actions such as saving to wishlist, sending host messages, updating price quotes, and starting bookings. It listens for outcomes from the various components and shows contextual feedback without making its own API calls.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; card width suggests using 3–4 columns on desktop with large outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom sequence as the mockup. At the very top, a full-width header band contains the global search on the left and user summary on the right. Below that, a centered main content container uses a left-focused composition: the listing’s photo gallery and core details stack vertically in a dominant left column, while booking-related controls (stay context, live quote, book CTA, wishlist toggle) sit in a narrower right column aligned to the top of the gallery. Supporting sections (host message form, map, policies) continue in the main content flow beneath the initial hero area, primarily occupying the left column or full width. Overlay elements (destination suggestions, wishlist dialog, notifications) float above this structure, anchored to their relevant triggers.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom sequence as the mockup. At the very top, a full-width header band contains the global search on the left and user summary on the right. Below that, a centered main content container uses a left-focused composition: the listing’s photo gallery and core details stack vertically in a dominant left column, while booking-related controls (stay context, live quote, book CTA, wishlist toggle) sit in a narrower right column aligned to the top of the gallery. Supporting sections (host message form, map, policies) continue in the main content flow beneath the initial hero area, primarily occupying the left column or full width. Overlay elements (destination suggestions, wishlist dialog, notifications) float above this structure, anchored to their relevant triggers.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header region (search + user summary) occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the initial viewport height, full width, but with a relatively shallow visual profile. The main content container uses a centered max-width layout (approx. 1140–1280px) with generous side gutters. Inside, the left column takes about 60–65% of the content width for the gallery and details, while the right column takes about 30–35% for booking controls, leaving visible whitespace between columns and at the right edge. Vertical spacing between distinct sections (header to gallery, gallery to details, details to map/policies) uses 32–48px margins, while cards and panels have 16–24px internal padding, maintaining the airy feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps information density intentionally low, mirroring the editorial, lifestyle tone of the mockup. Key elements (photo gallery, price summary, book CTA) are presented in large, simple blocks with plenty of whitespace around them. Text sections (policies, host message) are broken into readable chunks with generous line spacing and margins, avoiding dense walls of text. Only a few primary elements are visible above the fold: large imagery, a compact booking card, and essential listing details, with secondary controls tucked into lightweight overlays or subtle buttons.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow maintains the strong vertical, slightly left-biased F-pattern from the mockup. The eye starts at the header band (green-accented areas like active search state or notifications), moves right to the user summary, then drops into the main content where the large photo gallery on the left becomes the primary anchor. From the gallery, the gaze travels down to the listing title and core facts, then sweeps right to the booking panel (stay context, price quote, book CTA, wishlist toggle). Below, attention continues down the page through the host message form and map, ending at policies. Overlays (wishlist dialog, notifications, destination suggestions) briefly interrupt this flow in context, but are visually lightweight and aligned to edges to preserve the main vertical narrative.

layout.overall_description: The Listing Details page presents as a clean, centered canvas with a light, resort-like atmosphere. A full-width header band sits at the very top, with the global search field occupying the left half and the user summary and account controls aligned to the right, all on a mostly white background accented by the brand green in focus states and icons. Beneath this, the main content area appears as a large, white, softly shadowed zone floating on the page with generous left and right gutters. Within this container, the layout splits into two columns: a wide left column shows a large, edge-to-edge photo gallery card first, followed immediately below by the listing’s title, rating, location, and key details. To the right, a taller, compact booking card starts just below the top of the gallery, containing stay controls, a live price summary, and a prominent green 'Book Now' button, with a wishlist heart near its top edge. As the user scrolls, this right booking card remains visible (sticky) for much of the scroll, while the left column continues to reveal more textual detail, the host message form, a wide map card, and policy information stacked vertically. Throughout, 32–48px vertical gaps separate sections, and cards use 16–24px internal padding with rounded corners and soft shadows, echoing the relaxed, vacation-centric style. Occasional overlays—such as destination suggestions dropping under the search, feedback messages appearing in the top-right, and a centered wishlist dialog—layer above this structure without crowding it, preserving the spacious and focused experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search bar is positioned in the top header band, aligned to the left side of the page and spanning roughly 50–60% of the header’s width. It is centered vertically within a shallow header height (about 72–88px tall) with 16–24px of padding above and below, maintaining the airy spacing. The search field is a long, horizontally oriented pill or rounded rectangle with subtle shadow, using the brand green for the focused border or search icon. It sits in a straight line with the user summary on the right, creating a clean horizontal navigation row. The destination suggestions dropdown appears directly below this field, matching its width and left alignment, creating a strong vertical alignment from header to dropdown.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the top header; remains at the top of the page and scrolls off once the user moves down, unless global sticky header behavior is added elsewhere in the system.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The user summary occupies the top-right portion of the same header band as the global search. It is right-aligned within the content container, roughly taking up the final 20–25% of the header’s width. Elements (user avatar, name, or sign-in call to action) are arranged horizontally, with 8–16px gaps between icons and text, and ample right padding (24–32px) before the edge of the content container. Vertically, it is aligned with the centerline of the search bar to form a single, neat horizontal header row, with the surrounding whitespace keeping it visually light and unobtrusive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the header, scrolling away with the header as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The listing core details sit directly below the photo gallery in the main left column, aligning with the left edge of the gallery and occupying about 60–65% of the content container’s width. There is a 32–40px vertical gap between the bottom of the gallery and the top of the details block, continuing the spacious stack pattern seen in the mockup. Inside this block, the listing title appears at the top in a large, bold type with plenty of breathing room (around 16–24px padding above), followed by rating, review count, and location arranged in a left-aligned row or short stacked rows with subtle iconography in brand green. Essential facts (bedrooms, guests, amenities highlights) appear beneath, organized in 2–3 short lines or a tidy grid but still low-density, using plenty of horizontal spacing and line-height. The right booking column begins at approximately the same vertical level as these details but remains distinct, separated by 48–64px of horizontal space and a clear column division.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll; it scrolls behind the sticky booking column on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The photo gallery acts as a hero card at the top of the main content area, anchored to the left column and aligned to the same internal left margin as the listing details. It spans roughly 60–65% of the content width and has a fixed height ratio (for example, a 16:9 or slightly taller aspect) that makes it visually dominant without occupying the entire viewport height. The main image fills the card edge-to-edge with rounded corners and a soft shadow separating it from the white background. Thumbnails, arrows, or gallery controls (if present) are overlaid subtly along the bottom or right edge of the photo, kept minimal to preserve the clean image-first feel. There is approximately 32–48px of vertical whitespace between the header and the top of this gallery, establishing it as the main focal point once the header scrolls away.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; it scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The stay context controller—containing date pickers and guest selectors—is positioned within the booking card on the right side of the main content, near the top. The entire booking card is vertically aligned so that its top edge is roughly level with the bottom third of the photo gallery, and it occupies about 30–35% of the content width. Inside this card, the stay controller sits in the upper half, using stacked fields (dates above guests) or a compact grid in a vertical layout with 16–24px internal padding. The fields are full-width within the card, with rounded corners and subtle shadows or borders, maintaining the relaxed, minimal look. There is clear separation (16–24px vertical gap) between the stay fields and the price summary section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained within a booking card that behaves as a sticky sidebar on desktop: it stays pinned near the top-right of the viewport while the user scrolls through the left-column content, up to a defined footer or boundary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price quote panel is directly below the stay context fields inside the same right-column booking card, sharing the card’s 30–35% width. It is organized as a clean, vertical block, with a bold total price and per-night breakdown at the top, followed by a short, low-density list of line items (nightly rate, cleaning fee, taxes) in smaller text. Internal spacing is generous: 16–24px padding around the edges and 8–12px between each line item, ensuring the numbers are easy to scan and not cramped. The panel precedes the main 'Book Now' button, with about 16–24px of vertical spacing below the pricing section to separate it visually from the CTA. The brand green may be used sparingly for accent (e.g., total price or badges) to make this panel feel important but not heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives within the same sticky booking card on the right; it scrolls with the card as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'Book Now' call-to-action button sits at the bottom portion of the booking card in the right column, spanning nearly the full internal width of the card (leaving about 16px padding on left and right). It appears immediately beneath the price quote panel, separated by around 16–24px of vertical whitespace. The button is large and high-contrast, using the brand green fill with white text, rounded corners that match other elements, and a modest elevation shadow to give it priority. There is a small buffer of whitespace (16–24px) below the button to keep it from touching the bottom edge of the card, preserving the airy feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the sticky booking card; remains visible in the top-right area of the viewport while scrolling through the listing content, encouraging conversion without feeling intrusive.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist toggle appears as a small heart-shaped icon or compact pill button near the top of the booking card on the right, aligned to the top-right corner inside the card’s padding. It does not occupy a full row; instead, it floats in the card’s header area, with about 8–12px spacing from the card’s top and right interior edges. Alternatively, on some breakpoints, it can overlay the top-right corner of the main photo in the gallery, but it remains visually connected to the booking area through consistent styling (brand green or outline). Its size is modest—about 32–40px touch target—so it’s clearly interactive but not competing with the main 'Book Now' button.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Integrated into the sticky booking card; it stays visible with the booking card as the user scrolls. On mobile, it may float near the top of the screen with the booking bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist dialog appears as a centered modal overlay on top of the entire page when the wishlist toggle is activated. It is a medium-sized, rounded card occupying roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop and 60–70% on tablet, with a max width to keep content readable. Vertically, it is centered with generous margins from the viewport edges (at least 64px top and bottom), and a dim, slightly blurred backdrop covers the rest of the page to focus attention. Inside, the dialog uses 24px internal padding, with the title at the top, a list of wishlists or input fields below, and a primary action button aligned bottom-right or full-width at the bottom. The overall look echoes other cards: white background, soft shadow, rounded corners, low-density layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay in the center of the viewport; the underlying page is dimmed and non-interactive while the dialog is open. Scroll, if any, is contained within the dialog for longer content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The host message form is placed below the main listing details in the left column, aligned with the same left and right edges as the listing core details. There is a 32–48px vertical gap between the last detail section and the top of this form to clearly separate it as a new interaction area. The form appears as a light card or simple bordered block, with a short intro line or host avatar at the top, followed by a large, multi-line message textarea and a subtle submit button below. Internal padding is around 16–24px, and the fields span the full width of the left column to keep the experience simple and welcoming. This section is visually lighter than the booking card—maybe using outline styling instead of a strong shadow—to signal that messaging is optional and secondary to booking.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the main scrollable flow in the left column, moving behind the sticky booking card on the right on larger screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location map section appears further down the page, after the host message form, and transitions back to a more image-first block similar to the photo gallery. On desktop, it stretches across the full width of the content container, effectively bridging the left and right columns and resetting the layout into a single, wide card. There is a 32–48px vertical gap above the map, and inside, the map itself fills most of the card’s area with rounded corners and a soft shadow, echoing the gallery styling. A simple title or label (e.g., 'Location') sits above or overlaid at the top-left, kept minimal so the map remains the dominant visual element. Surrounding whitespace on all sides keeps the section from feeling heavy despite its width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width section within the main scroll; the sticky booking card no longer overlaps it once the page scrolls beyond the sticky boundary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cancellation and policies section is positioned beneath the map, returning to a more text-centric area that still respects the low-density design. It occupies the full width of the content container but uses a narrower text column (around 60–70% of the width centered or left-aligned) to keep lines readable, with ample margins on either side. There is 32–48px vertical spacing between the map and this section. Within the section, headings such as 'Cancellation Policy' and 'House Rules' are clearly separated with 24–32px spacing, and each block of text is broken into short paragraphs or bullet points with generous line-height. The styling is simple—light dividers or subtle background tint—but consistent with the rest of the site’s minimal, relaxed aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the main flow; it forms part of the natural scroll towards the bottom of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The destination suggestions dropdown is tightly coupled with the header-global-search component. When the user types into the search field, this dropdown appears directly underneath it, left-aligned to the search field and matching its width, creating one continuous vertical block. It extends downward over the top portion of the main content area if needed, with a maximum height (e.g., 40–50% of viewport height) and internal scrolling for long lists. Each suggestion is presented as a spacious, single-line or two-line row with 8–12px vertical padding, light separators, and subtle highlight states in brand green on hover or focus. The dropdown maintains the same rounded corners and soft shadow as other cards, reinforcing the design system.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Floating but anchored: it appears directly beneath the search bar, overlaying the content below. It scrolls away with the header if the user scrolls while it’s open or closes when focus leaves the search field.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notifications center appears as transient toasts or a small panel in the upper-right portion of the viewport, visually aligned with the header’s right area but floating above content. Individual notifications are compact cards about 280–340px wide, stacking vertically with 8–12px gaps, and they use the brand green sparingly for icons or success states. They are inset from the right and top edges by about 24–32px to avoid touching the browser chrome, and include 12–16px internal padding and rounded corners with a soft shadow. When multiple messages are present (e.g., wishlist saved, booking error), they appear as a short stack, with new messages appearing at the top and older ones sliding down.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed position in the top-right of the viewport; it overlays the header and content while visible, then auto-dismisses or can be manually dismissed, without disrupting the main vertical scroll.

name: Checkout & Booking
title: Checkout & Booking
description: Multi-step page to review an in-progress vacation rental booking, enter guest details, optionally apply a promo code, choose a payment option, and complete the booking with payment details.

core_features: Load and display the active booking and associated rental property details using getActiveBookingSummary, including dates, guests, and itemized price breakdown.
Show a compact header with user summary using getHeaderUserSummary, including basic profile info and wishlist indicators.
Collect and update guest contact information (first name, last name, email, phone) for the active booking using updateActiveBookingGuestInfo.
Allow the user to enter and apply a promo code to the active booking via applyPromoCodeToActiveBooking and immediately reflect updated pricing.
Let the user select a payment option for the booking (e.g., full payment now or split payment if supported) using setPaymentOptionForActiveBooking.
Advance the booking from guest details to the payment step without requiring immediate payment details using proceedToPaymentStep.
Collect payment details on the payment step and finalize the booking using finalizeBookingWithPaymentDetails.
Provide an inline destination search panel that suggests destinations using getDestinationSuggestions and starts a new search using startNewSearch.
Support navigation back to the home page (index.html) and to other pages via standard links with URL parameters.
user_workflows: Complete booking from guest details to confirmation: 1) User arrives on checkout.html with a bookingId in the URL. 2) Page calls getHeaderUserSummary to render the header and getActiveBookingSummary to load the active booking and rental. 3) User reviews the booking summary (dates, guests, price items) and, if needed, edits guest details in the guest-info form. On submit, updateActiveBookingGuestInfo is called and its response is used to update the local booking state. 4) User optionally enters a promo code and clicks Apply; applyPromoCodeToActiveBooking is called and, on success, the booking price breakdown is updated on screen. 5) User selects a payment option (full or split if available); setPaymentOptionForActiveBooking is called, and the returned booking is used to update the selected option and any payment breakdown shown. 6) User clicks Continue to payment; proceedToPaymentStep is called and, on success, the UI step state switches to the payment_details step. 7) On the payment step, user fills in payment details and submits; finalizeBookingWithPaymentDetails is called. 8) If successful, the booking status is updated (e.g., to confirmed), and the UI switches to a read-only confirmation view with the final booking summary.

Apply and manage promo codes: 1) While on the guest-details step, user enters a promo code into the promo form and submits. 2) applyPromoCodeToActiveBooking is called with the entered code. 3) If success is true, the returned booking object with updated price_promo_discount_amount and price_total_before_taxes replaces the current booking state, and the summary reflects the discount. 4) If success is false, an error message from the response is displayed, and the previous pricing remains unchanged. 5) User can try another code; each attempt triggers applyPromoCodeToActiveBooking anew and re-renders the booking summary.

Start a new rental search from checkout: 1) User opens the inline search panel on the checkout page. 2) As the user types a destination name, getDestinationSuggestions is called with the query and the suggestions list updates. 3) User selects a suggested destination, chooses dates and guest counts, and submits the search form. 4) startNewSearch is called with the selected destination and the entered dates and guest counts. 5) On success, the returned searchContext and results are displayed inline (e.g., a count and a short result list), and the user is provided normal navigation links to move to other pages (such as a listing page) using URL parameters if they choose to abandon the current checkout.
interactions: On page load, read the bookingId from the URL and call getHeaderUserSummary and getActiveBookingSummary to initialize the header and booking summary.
If getActiveBookingSummary indicates there is no active booking or it does not match the bookingId URL parameter, show an error state with a link to index.html.

When the user edits guest details and submits the guest-info form, call updateActiveBookingGuestInfo; on success, update the booking state and clear any validation errors; on failure, show the returned message.

When the user enters a promo code and clicks Apply, call applyPromoCodeToActiveBooking; on success, update the booking state (including any promo-related fields) and re-render the price breakdown; on failure, show an inline error without changing the pricing.

When the user selects a payment option (e.g., by changing a radio button), call setPaymentOptionForActiveBooking; on success, update the booking state and reflect the selected option; on failure, revert the UI selection and show an error.

When the user clicks Continue to payment, call proceedToPaymentStep; if success is true and nextStep is 'payment_details', set the current UI step to the payment step; if unsuccessful, show an error and keep the user on the guest-details step.

On the payment step, when the user submits payment details, call finalizeBookingWithPaymentDetails; on success, update the booking state and switch the UI to a confirmation view; on failure, keep the user on the payment step and display the error message.
While typing in the destination search field in the inline search panel, call getDestinationSuggestions (with debouncing) and update a suggestions list for the user to pick from.

When the user submits the inline search form with a selected destination and dates, call startNewSearch; on success, use the returned searchContext and results to display a compact results summary and links to other pages that include appropriate URL parameters.

At any time, when the user clicks the logo or a Back to Home link, navigate to index.html using standard anchor navigation (optionally preserving or discarding the current URL parameters according to global navigation rules).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page reads a bookingId URL parameter on load (e.g., checkout.html?bookingId={id}) and uses it as the expected active booking identifier. It immediately calls getHeaderUserSummary and getActiveBookingSummary. If getActiveBookingSummary.hasActiveBooking is false or the returned booking.id does not match the bookingId parameter, the page enters an error state where the booking-specific components are hidden and only an error message and navigation options (such as Back to Home) are shown. If a valid booking is returned, its data (Booking and associated RentalProperty) populate the internal page state. The UI maintains a local 'currentStep' state, typically 'guest_details', 'payment_details', or 'confirmation'. Initial currentStep is determined by the booking: if booking.is_payment_details_entered is true or booking.status is confirmed, the page can start in a confirmation-like state; otherwise it starts at 'guest_details'. When proceedToPaymentStep returns success with nextStep equal to 'payment_details', the currentStep is updated accordingly. When finalizeBookingWithPaymentDetails succeeds, booking.status is expected to transition (for example, to confirmed) and is used to lock the UI into a read-only confirmation state. Booking fields such as promo_code_applied, promo_code_input, price_promo_discount_amount, price_long_stay_discount_amount, price_nights_subtotal, price_cleaning_fee, price_service_fee, price_other_fees, price_total_before_taxes, payment_option, and currency drive what is displayed in the booking summary and payment-option UI. The availability of split payment is determined by the RentalProperty.supports_split_payment_50_50 flag from getActiveBookingSummary; if false, the split option is not presented. The inline search panel maintains its own local state (destination query text, selected destination, dates, and guest counts). As query text changes, getDestinationSuggestions is called; when the search form is submitted, startNewSearch is called and its SearchContext and results are stored in local state only for display on this page. No state is persisted in localStorage; all persistence relies on the server-side Booking and SearchContext records returned by the assigned interfaces and on URL parameters for navigation.

components.functionality: Displays the site header for the checkout page, including a logo link to index.html, a minimal profile summary, and wishlist count indicators, based entirely on the getHeaderUserSummary response.

Shows the active booking and rental details returned by getActiveBookingSummary, including property title, location, check-in/check-out dates, nights, guest counts, and the itemized price breakdown (nightly subtotal, cleaning, service, other fees, long-stay discount, promo discount, total before taxes). It updates whenever booking-related interfaces return an updated Booking.

Collects guest_first_name, guest_last_name, guest_email, and guest_phone for the active booking. Pre-fills its fields from the current Booking values when available. On submit, it calls updateActiveBookingGuestInfo and updates the booking state and any validation messages based on the response.

Allows the user to enter a promo code for the active booking. The input is optionally pre-filled with booking.promo_code_input. When the user submits, it calls applyPromoCodeToActiveBooking with the entered code and, on success, updates the Booking state (including promo_code_applied, price_promo_discount_amount, and price_total_before_taxes) and re-renders the booking summary.

Presents available payment options for the active booking. It inspects Booking.payment_option and RentalProperty.supports_split_payment_50_50 to determine which options to show and which is currently selected. When the user changes the selection, it calls setPaymentOptionForActiveBooking and updates the Booking state based on the response.

Controls the multi-step flow on the page (guest_details, payment_details, confirmation). It initializes currentStep based on the Booking status and is_payment_details_entered, shows or hides the relevant components per step, and coordinates the transition from guest_details to payment_details via proceedToPaymentStep and from payment_details to confirmation via finalizeBookingWithPaymentDetails.

Collects the user's payment details during the payment_details step and submits them to finalize the booking. It constructs a paymentDetails object from the form inputs and passes it to finalizeBookingWithPaymentDetails. On success, the returned Booking (with updated status and is_payment_details_entered) is stored and the UI moves to a confirmation state.

Provides an inline search capability for users who wish to start a new rental search from the checkout page. As the user types into the destination input, it calls getDestinationSuggestions to display suggested Destination options. When the user selects a destination, enters dates and guest counts, and submits the form, it calls startNewSearch and displays the returned SearchContext and a compact list or count of RentalProperty results on this page.

Displays a read-only summary of the booking after finalizeBookingWithPaymentDetails succeeds or if the page is loaded with a Booking that is already confirmed. It shows booking details and a confirmation status based entirely on the Booking and RentalProperty data and disables any further editing or payment actions.
Provides a simple link (for example, in the header or in an error state) that navigates the user back to index.html, without invoking any additional interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; card width suggests using 3–4 columns on desktop with large outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a vertical, top-to-bottom flow with a full-width header at the very top (contact strip + logo/nav combined into the header-bar), followed by a centered main content container. Inside this container, all checkout content is organized in a single left-aligned column that occupies roughly 40–50% of the content width on desktop, mirroring the narrow property-card column in the mockup. Components are stacked in a clear sequence: navigation/back + step controller at the top of the column, then inline destination search, then the booking summary, followed by the relevant step’s form content (guest info, payment options/details, promo code) or confirmation summary, all aligned to the same left column. The right side of the content area remains largely open, echoing the mockup’s generous blank space to the right of the cards.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a vertical, top-to-bottom flow with a full-width header at the very top (contact strip + logo/nav combined into the header-bar), followed by a centered main content container. Inside this container, all checkout content is organized in a single left-aligned column that occupies roughly 40–50% of the content width on desktop, mirroring the narrow property-card column in the mockup. Components are stacked in a clear sequence: navigation/back + step controller at the top of the column, then inline destination search, then the booking summary, followed by the relevant step’s form content (guest info, payment options/details, promo code) or confirmation summary, all aligned to the same left column. The right side of the content area remains largely open, echoing the mockup’s generous blank space to the right of the cards.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header-bar uses the full browser width and roughly the top 20–25% of the initial viewport height (a shallow contact strip on top and a taller logo/nav row beneath). Below it, a main content area extends downwards, with a centered max-width container (~1120–1200px). Within that, the left checkout column spans about 4–5 of 12 grid columns (around 35–45% of the container width), leaving the remaining 7–8 columns as white space on the right. Vertical gaps of 32–48px separate major blocks (navigation/steps, search, summary, forms, confirmation), while individual elements within cards and forms have 16–24px padding and spacing, preserving the airy, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low. Each functional group (summary, each form, confirmation) sits inside a distinct white card with soft shadows and 16–24px internal padding. Only one main task area is visually active at a time within the column (e.g., guest info form or payment details), reducing cognitive load. Plenty of whitespace surrounds the left column and between components, with no crowded sidebars or dense multi-column forms. Labels, fields, and controls are spaced using multiples of 8px (primarily 16px and 24px), keeping the layout relaxed and breathable.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow is a top-to-bottom, slightly left-weighted F-pattern. The eye first hits the brand-colored elements in the header-bar, then moves down to the left-aligned back-to-home link and checkout step controller. From there, it descends to the inline destination search (as a low-friction, supporting control), then to the booking summary card (image/title/price-style information) and onward to the primary action areas: guest info, promo code, payment option selector, and payment details forms. Key interactive elements (step indicators, primary CTAs, key labels) use the brand green accent, guiding attention while maintaining the minimal aesthetic.

layout.overall_description: The Checkout & Booking page sits beneath a full-width, two-row header-bar that echoes the mockup’s contact strip and logo/navigation band: a slim green contact bar on top, and a slightly taller white nav bar with the Hawaii-inspired logo and simple text links below. Under this, the main content is centered in a wide container but visually anchored to the left, where a single narrow column of cards and forms runs vertically down the page. At the top of this column, a subtle back-to-home text link and a minimal step controller establish context for the multi-step flow. Just below, an inline destination search card gently reinforces the theme of Hawaiian escapes without competing for attention. The booking summary panel, styled like the original property card with a prominent image and title/price area, sits next and becomes the key reference point for all subsequent steps. Beneath it, the active step’s form (guest info, payment options/details, or promo code entry) appears as a clean, spacious white card with clear grouping and comfortable spacing. On completion, a booking confirmation summary card replaces the forms in the same left column, maintaining the same width and rhythm. Throughout, the right half of the content area remains largely empty, acting as breathing room that enhances the relaxed, resort-like feel of the experience, while the brand green accents guide the user’s eye down the page in a calm, controlled flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-bar spans the full width of the browser at the very top of the page and is composed of two horizontal bands, mirroring the mockup’s structure. The upper band is a shallow contact strip, approximately 10–12% of the combined header height, with a solid brand green background. Contact information (phone, email) and possibly a subtle callout (e.g., “24/7 island support”) are left-aligned within a centered max-width container, with tight but readable spacing to maintain its compact, utility feel. Directly beneath it, the lower band is a white nav bar occupying the remaining 88–90% of the header height. Inside the same centered container, the logo (script-style with palm icon) is left-aligned, and primary navigation links (e.g., Packages, Stays, Experiences) are horizontally aligned to the right or slightly right-of-center. The active page label is underlined in brand green, visually echoing the mockup’s active navigation underline. A soft bottom shadow delineates the header-bar from the main content. The header-bar height is roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height on desktop, shrinking proportionally on smaller screens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow (non-sticky). On scroll, it moves off-screen with the content. It always uses full-width background bands with content centered in a fixed-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-summary-panel sits within the main content container, left-aligned, and positioned below the back-to-home link, checkout-step-controller, and inline-destination-search-panel. There is a generous vertical gap of about 32–40px above it, separating it clearly from the search panel. The panel itself is styled as a card that visually parallels the property card from the mockup: at the top of the card, a full-width image (e.g., of the selected rental or a scenic Hawaii view) occupies roughly the top half of the card’s vertical space. Below the image, a white content block with 20–24px padding contains the rental title, key dates, guest count, and a prominent price/total cost area. The title is left-aligned and given typographic emphasis, while the price/total is also left-aligned but slightly separated by 8–16px of vertical space, using brand green for key numbers or labels. The entire card spans about 4–5 of 12 grid columns (approx. 40% of the container width) on desktop, matching the width of adjacent forms and the confirmation summary, and sits with substantial whitespace to its right. A soft shadow and rounded corners reinforce the relaxed, card-based aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; scrolls with the content. On long pages, it scrolls off-screen as the user moves down to subsequent forms. On smaller screens, it adapts to full-width and remains stacked in the same sequence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guest-info-form appears directly below the booking-summary-panel, separated by approximately 32–48px of vertical space to maintain clear segmentation. It is contained within a white card that matches the summary panel’s width and styling: left-aligned within the content container, spanning about 4–5 columns of the 12-column grid (roughly 40–45% of the container width) on desktop, with ample white space to the right. Inside the card, there is 20–24px of internal padding on all sides. The form title (e.g., “Guest details”) is left-aligned at the top, followed by a short descriptive line if needed, and then stacked form fields (name, email, phone, address, etc.) arranged in a single column or simple two-up rows for shorter fields, with 16px vertical spacing between fields. Group headings (like “Primary guest” vs “Additional guests”) have a bit more space above (24px) to form subtle sub-sections. The primary action button (e.g., “Continue to payment”) sits aligned to the left at the bottom of the card, with a comfortable 24–32px margin above it, and uses the brand green for prominence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked within the main left column. It appears only during the relevant step of the checkout process, replacing other step-specific forms while maintaining the same spatial footprint. On mobile, it becomes full-width with the same vertical stacking pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promo-code-form is positioned in the main content column beneath the guest-info-form or, in flows where promo code entry is tied to payment, beneath the payment-option-selector but before the full payment-details-form. It is separated from the component above by about 24–32px, giving it slightly less prominence than the major forms but still clear visual separation. The promo code area is a compact white card with the same width as the other cards in the left column (4–5 of 12 grid columns), but with a slightly reduced height. Inside, there is 16–20px of padding. A small, left-aligned heading or label (e.g., “Have a promo code?”) sits at the top, followed by a single-line text input and an adjacent or inline “Apply” button. The field and button are left-aligned, with the button in brand green, and the entire arrangement feels spacious with 16px gaps. Applied code status and savings are shown in a subtle line below the field, still within the card’s bounds. The right side of the card remains clean with generous white space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column and stacked in the flow. It appears only when relevant (e.g., during pricing/payment steps), and on smaller screens it expands to full width while maintaining the same vertical spacing logic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment-option-selector is placed in the main content column in the payment step, directly above the payment-details-form. It sits about 32px below either the booking-summary-panel (if the payment step appears immediately after summary) or the preceding form (e.g., guest-info-form). The selector is implemented as a horizontal or vertically stacked set of pill-like or card-like options (e.g., credit card, PayPal, split payment) within a white card that aligns with the same left column width (4–5 of 12 grid columns). The card has 20–24px internal padding and rounded corners with a soft shadow consistent with other cards. Each payment option is left-aligned inside this card, with a radio button or toggle marker on the left and label text to its right, separated by 8–12px. Options are vertically stacked with 12–16px between them. The currently selected option is highlighted with a subtle brand green border or background tint. This entire selector block visually precedes the more detailed payment form that appears directly beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and stacked in the left column. It remains visible just above the payment-details-form as long as the payment step is active. On small screens, options may reflow but still remain within a single stacked card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-step-controller appears near the top of the main content column, directly beneath the header-bar and just below (or to the right of) the back-to-home-link. There is a 32–40px vertical gap between the bottom of the header-bar and this controller, providing breathing room as the first in-content element. It is left-aligned and spans roughly the same width as the column of cards below (4–5 of 12 grid columns), but visually lighter than a full card—either as a slim horizontal stepper or a minimal segmented control. Steps (e.g., 1. Review, 2. Guest details, 3. Payment, 4. Confirmation) are arranged left to right, with text labels and possibly small numbered circles. The active step is highlighted using brand green (underline, filled step badge, or both), echoing the mockup’s active navigation underline. The controller has 8–16px of vertical space within it and may rest on an invisible or very subtle line to anchor it visually. Below it, with 24–32px of vertical space, the inline-destination-search-panel begins, tying the sequence together.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky, sitting at the top of the content flow. As the user scrolls down through long forms, it scrolls out of view. On small screens, step labels may wrap or compress but the controller stays left-aligned with the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment-details-form is positioned directly below the payment-option-selector (and below the promo-code-form when the promo code is integrated with payment), with a 24–32px vertical gap separating it from the selector. It is one of the larger and more information-dense cards, but still follows the airy, single-column, left-aligned pattern. The card spans about 4–5 of 12 grid columns on desktop, flush with the left margin of the content container, and has 20–24px padding on all sides. Inside, fields like card number, expiration date, CVV, billing address, and name on card are arranged in a primarily vertical stack with occasional simple 2-column rows (e.g., expiration + CVV) to keep the form compact without feeling cramped. Each field has around 12–16px vertical spacing. A security reassurance line (e.g., lock icon + “Secure payment”) sits near the bottom, left-aligned. The primary payment button (e.g., “Complete booking”) is positioned at the bottom left of the card, with 24–32px of space above it, styled in brand green and sized to fit naturally within the card width rather than stretching edge-to-edge. The right side of the card and the page maintain generous empty space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and stacked within the left column. It appears only when the user reaches the payment step, replacing other step-specific forms while the booking-summary-panel remains in place above. On smaller screens it stretches to full width but preserves the same vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline-destination-search-panel sits beneath the checkout-step-controller and above the booking-summary-panel, acting as a contextual search/adjustment control without overshadowing the main checkout flow. It is separated from the step controller by about 24px of vertical space and from the booking summary by 32–40px. Styled as a low-profile white card with a soft shadow, it aligns with the left column and spans 4–5 of 12 grid columns on desktop. Inside, with about 16–20px padding, there is a single, prominent search input (e.g., destination or dates) and possibly one or two secondary filters (like dates or guests) arranged inline or stacked depending on space. The search field is left-aligned, with a faint border and a subtle search icon on the left, mimicking the restrained minimalism of the mockup’s controls. The card feels lighter and less tall than the booking-summary-panel, signifying its supportive role in the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked near the top of the left column. It scrolls off-screen as the user moves down through summary and forms. On mobile, it becomes full-width, maintaining its relative positioning between the step controller and summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-confirmation-summary occupies the same spatial position and width as the guest-info-form or payment-details-form but appears only after the booking is successfully completed. It is positioned directly below the booking-summary-panel, with 32–40px vertical space between them, in keeping with the established rhythm. This confirmation card spans about 4–5 of 12 columns, left-aligned in the content container, and uses the same rounded, soft-shadowed card style with 20–24px internal padding. At the top, a clear visual confirmation element (e.g., a subtle green check icon and a headline like “Booking confirmed!”) is left-aligned and given more typographic weight. Below, the key confirmation details (reservation number, dates, guest list, payment summary) are presented in a clean vertical list, with section headings and 12–16px vertical spacing between rows. A secondary action area at the bottom (buttons like “View itinerary” or “Print confirmation”) is left-aligned and uses brand green for primary actions, but remains compact to preserve the calm tone. The right side remains empty, echoing the rest of the page’s spacious composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, replacing step-specific forms once the process is complete. It scrolls naturally with the rest of the page. On small screens it becomes full-width while retaining its stacked, vertical layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-home-link is placed at the top of the main content area, directly under the header-bar and aligned with the left margin of the main content column. It appears slightly above and to the left of the checkout-step-controller, with about 8–12px of vertical space between the link and the step controller, or they may sit on the same horizontal line with the link on the far left and the step controller starting a bit to the right. The link is simple text (e.g., “← Back to home”) using a muted gray or low-saturation green, with no card or background of its own, to keep it visually lightweight. Its left alignment anchors the user at the start of the content column, reinforcing the F-pattern. A 32–40px gap separates this area from the header above, ensuring the link doesn’t feel crowded by the navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline at the top of the content flow. It scrolls off-screen as the user moves down. On mobile, it remains positioned at the top of the stack, above the step controller and other components, still left-aligned.

name: Account & Profile
title: Account & Profile
description: Manage basic account details and travel preferences, including preferred destination and contact information, and access related account areas such as wishlists and help.

core_features: Load and display the current profile using getProfile, or show an empty form if no profile exists.
Allow the user to create or update their profile (name, email, password, phone, preferred destination) using createOrUpdateProfile.
Provide an assisted preferred-destination field with autocomplete suggestions using getDestinationSuggestions.
Show header account and wishlist summary (profile presence, display name, default wishlist indicator) using getHeaderUserSummary.
Offer navigation shortcuts from the account area to wishlists.html and help.html.
Allow the user to initiate a new rental search from the profile page based on their chosen destination and guest details using startNewSearch, and optionally navigate to search_results.html.
Support submitting a search via the global header search while on this page, passing search parameters via URL to search_results.html.
user_workflows:
View or create profile:
1. User navigates to profile.html, optionally with a source URL parameter (e.g., profile.html?source=home_sign_up or profile.html?source=header_account).
2. On page initialization, the page calls getHeaderUserSummary to populate the header summary, then calls getProfile.
3. If getProfile.hasProfile is false, the Account & Profile form is rendered empty for account creation.
4. If getProfile.hasProfile is true, all available profile fields (name, email, phone, preferred_destination, preferred_destination_id) are populated into the form.
5. The user can adjust any field and save, which triggers createOrUpdateProfile; the returned Profile is then used to refresh the form and header summary state.

Edit and save basic account details:
1. User edits the name, email, password, or phone fields in the profile form.
2. When the user submits the form, the component validates locally (e.g., required email format where needed) without using external APIs beyond those provided.
3. The page calls createOrUpdateProfile with the current field values, including preferredDestination and preferredDestinationId from the preferred destination field state.
4. On success, the form updates with the returned Profile data, and a confirmation state (e.g., non-visual flag) is set so that subsequent edits continue treating this as an update rather than a creation.
5. If the page is reloaded later, getProfile will now return hasProfile = true and the saved Profile fields.

Set preferred destination with suggestions:
1. User focuses the preferred destination text input in the profile form.
2. As the user types into the preferred destination field, the component calls getDestinationSuggestions with the current query string after debounce and only when the query is non-empty.
3. The returned list of Destination entities is rendered as suggestions.
4. The user selects a suggestion; the component sets the preferredDestination text value to the selected Destination.name and stores preferredDestinationId as the selected Destination.id.
5. If the user clears or manually edits the text so that it no longer matches a selected suggestion, the component keeps preferredDestination as the free text, and clears preferredDestinationId so that createOrUpdateProfile only persists the free-text preference.
6. On profile form submit, createOrUpdateProfile is called with both preferredDestination and the currently stored preferredDestinationId (if any).

Use profile preferences to start a new search from the profile page:
1. User scrolls to the search-from-profile section that uses their preferred destination.
2. The component reads the current profile state (already loaded via getProfile) and pre-fills the destination field with profile.preferred_destination and, if available, binds preferred_destination_id as the selected destination.
3. User inputs or adjusts check-in, check-out, and guest counts (adults, children, pets) via a small search form specific to this page.
4. On search form submit in this section, the component calls startNewSearch with destinationId (if available or selected), checkIn, checkOut, adultsCount, childrenCount, and petsCount.
5. The component receives a searchContext and an initial results array from startNewSearch.
6. The component optionally displays a minimal confirmation or a link/button like "View search results", which when clicked navigates to search_results.html with destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and pets encoded as URL parameters derived from the local search form state (not from static values).
7. Alternatively, the user may choose to stay on the profile page; no navigation is forced.

Navigate to wishlists and help from the account area:
1. User locates the account links section on the profile page.
2. A link or button labeled for wishlists is available; when clicked, the browser navigates directly to wishlists.html (no URL parameters are required by this spec).
3. Another link or button labeled for help/support is available; when clicked, the browser navigates directly to help.html.
4. These navigations do not require additional API calls from this page; they only use the existing navigation configuration.

Use global header search while on the profile page:
1. While viewing profile.html, user interacts with the global header search component (destination, dates, guest counts) that is consistent across the site.
2. As the user enters a destination in the header search, that search component may call getDestinationSuggestions with the query to power its autocomplete (using the same interface as the profile preferred destination field).
3. On header search submit, the header search component assembles the parameters: headerSearchDestination.id, headerSearchDestination.name, headerSearchCheckInISO8601, headerSearchCheckOutISO8601, headerSearchAdultsCount, headerSearchChildrenCount, headerSearchPetsCount.
4. The page navigates to search_results.html with these values passed as URL parameters: destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets.
5. The search_results.html page can then use startNewSearch based on those URL parameters to initialize search; this profile page does not need to use startNewSearch for this header search workflow.

Behavior based on source URL param:
1. User opens profile.html with or without a source query parameter (e.g., profile.html or profile.html?source=home_sign_up).
2. On initialization, the page reads the source URL parameter.
3. If source indicates an intent to sign up (e.g., a value like home_sign_up), the page prioritizes and focuses the core profile form fields (e.g., emphasizing email and password inputs) and may scroll them into view.
4. If source indicates navigation from an existing account link (e.g., header_account), the page may emphasize profile review, preferred destination, and account links sections.
5. The source parameter is not persisted beyond the current URL; it is interpreted only at load time to adjust initial focus and potentially copy variations such as hint text in the form.
interactions:
Initial page load:
- onInit: Call getHeaderUserSummary to populate header user info and wishlist count.
- onInit: Call getProfile to determine whether a profile exists and to populate the profile form fields.
- onInit: Read the source URL parameter and set an internal pageMode state used to adjust which section is focused or emphasized (e.g., account creation vs. editing).

Editing text fields in profile form (name, email, password, phone):
- onChange: Update in-memory form state for each field.
- onBlur (optional): Perform lightweight validation checks (e.g., email format) and store validation state in memory; no external calls beyond the provided APIs.
- onSubmit: If validation passes, call createOrUpdateProfile with the current values and any preferred destination state, then update the form with the returned Profile.

Preferred destination autocomplete in profile form:
- onInput: When the preferred destination text input value changes and is non-empty, trigger a debounced call to getDestinationSuggestions(query) with the current input.
- onSuggestionsReceived: Replace any existing suggestions list with the new Destination array.
- onSuggestionClick: Set the preferred destination text field to the clicked Destination.name and store that Destination.id in the internal preferredDestinationId state.
- onTextManualChange (after selecting a suggestion): If the user changes the text such that it no longer matches the selected suggestion, clear preferredDestinationId while keeping the text value.
- onFormSubmit: Pass both preferredDestination (text) and preferredDestinationId (if set) into createOrUpdateProfile.

Saving the profile:
- onSubmit (profile form): Collect name, email, password, phone, preferredDestination, preferredDestinationId from form state.
- onSubmit: Call createOrUpdateProfile with those arguments.
- onSuccess: Replace the current form state with the returned Profile object fields; mark hasProfile as true if it was previously false; optionally trigger a light success state.
- onError (if supported in runtime): Display a non-visual error state (e.g., binding an error message property) based on failure; no static messages are hardcoded beyond generic labels.

Navigating to wishlists:
- onClick (View wishlists link/button in account links section): Navigate browser to wishlists.html with no additional parameters from this page.
- This interaction does not invoke any of the page's assigned interfaces.

Navigating to help/support:
- onClick (Help/Support link/button in account links section): Navigate browser to help.html.
- This interaction does not invoke any of the page's assigned interfaces.

Using search-from-profile helper (internal search based on preferences):
- onInit: Use getProfile data, if available, to pre-fill the destination field and store any preferred_destination_id as the default destination selection.
- onDestinationInput: As user types destination in this helper, call getDestinationSuggestions(query) to provide destination suggestions (same pattern as the preferred destination field).
- onDestinationSuggestionSelect: Store selected destination.id and destination.name in internal state for the search form.
- onDatesChange: When user selects or changes check-in and check-out values, update local search form state; no external API is called yet.
- onGuestCountChange: Update adultsCount, childrenCount, petsCount in local search form state.
- onSubmit: Call startNewSearch with destinationId, checkIn, checkOut, adultsCount, childrenCount, petsCount from the local state.
- onSearchSuccess: Store returned searchContext in internal state (e.g., lastProfileSearchContext) and optionally show a control to go to search_results.html using the same parameters; this component does not alter URL parameters on its own except when performing navigation to search_results.html.
- onViewAllResultsClick: Navigate to search_results.html?destinationId=...&destinationName=...&checkIn=...&checkOut=...&adults=...&children=...&pets=... using values from the search form state.

Using global header search on this page:
- onHeaderDestinationInput: Header search field calls getDestinationSuggestions(query) to provide destination suggestions.
- onHeaderDestinationSuggestionSelect: Header search stores selected destination.id and destination.name locally.
- onHeaderSearchSubmit: Header search compiles destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets and triggers navigation to search_results.html with these as URL parameters; this submission does not call startNewSearch on this profile page.

Reacting to URL parameter changes (manual navigation or link changes):
- onURLChange (when profile.html is loaded or reloaded with a different source parameter): Re-run initialization logic (getHeaderUserSummary and getProfile) and adjust the internal pageMode based on source.
- The profile data itself is not tied to source; only the initial focus and behavior are influenced by it.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The profile.html page derives its behavior from the combination of URL parameters, data returned from assigned interfaces, and in-memory component state, without using localStorage or other persistent client-side storage. The primary URL parameter for this page is `source`.

1. URL Parameters:
- `source` (string, optional): Read on page initialization. This is not written or modified by the page; it is provided by the navigation that brings the user here. The page interprets it to set an internal `pageMode` state such as `"sign_up_intent"` or `"manage_account"` (values determined by comparison against the received string). This `pageMode` can be used to adjust:
  - which section is scrolled into view first (core profile form vs. account links),
  - which field receives initial focus (e.g., email for sign-up intent),
  - minor variations in instructional or hint text (expressed as dynamic bindings rather than hardcoded, context-specific strings).
  This value is not persisted across navigation except insofar as it remains in the browser URL; reloading the page will re-evaluate `source`.

2. Profile state:
- On load, getProfile is called. If `hasProfile` is false, an internal state flag (e.g., `hasProfile = false`) is set and the form is initialized with empty values. If `hasProfile` is true, the returned `profile` object populates the form state (name, email, password, phone, preferred_destination, preferred_destination_id).
- After any call to createOrUpdateProfile, the returned Profile replaces the in-memory profile state and sets `hasProfile = true`. This state is not pushed into URL parameters; it is only stored in memory until the user navigates away.
- The `preferred_destination_id` is used internally to know if the preferred destination maps to a known Destination.

3. Destination suggestion state:
- Destination suggestions are not stored in URL parameters. Instead, they are maintained as in-memory arrays scoped to each component that uses getDestinationSuggestions (profile preferred destination field and header/global search, if applicable).
- Each time the user types a query, a call is made with the query string. The returned Destination array replaces the current suggestions list. The selected suggestion's id and name are stored in component state (e.g., `selectedDestinationId` and `selectedDestinationName`) but are not persisted to the URL.

4. Search state from profile page:
- The search-from-profile helper maintains a transient search form state: destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adultsCount, childrenCount, petsCount. These values are initially derived from the current Profile where possible (particularly the preferred destination) but can be overwritten by the user.
- When startNewSearch is called, the returned SearchContext and results are stored in a temporary component-level state (e.g., `lastProfileSearchContext`). This is not added to the URL and is only used for immediate feedback or to inform the user that a search has been started.
- When the user opts to view the full results, the page navigates to search_results.html with the search parameters encoded into the URL as `destinationId`, `destinationName`, `checkIn`, `checkOut`, `adults`, `children`, `pets`, using the search form state rather than storing identifier references in local storage.

5. Header search state:
- The global header search component maintains its own in-memory state for destination and guest selection. Upon submission from this page, it directly navigates to search_results.html using URL parameters (destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets). The profile.html page does not persist any of this state beyond the navigation.

6. Header summary state:
- getHeaderUserSummary is called on initialization to determine whether a profile exists (hasProfile), the profileName for display, and the defaultWishlistId and defaultWishlistRentalCount. These values are only used to render header UI (account link text and wishlist indicator) and are not persisted or encoded in URL parameters. Any subsequent update to the profile via createOrUpdateProfile can optionally trigger a re-fetch of getHeaderUserSummary to keep header display consistent, but that is handled in memory.

Overall, the source URL parameter influences initial focus and context; search-related parameters only appear in the URL when navigating away (to search_results.html) and not while staying on profile.html; profile and suggestion data is maintained only in memory and via the APIs, not in URL parameters or localStorage.

components.functionality: Fetches and displays a concise summary of the user's account status and default wishlist for the global page header, including indicators such as whether a profile exists, a display name, and a wishlist count. Provides access to the Account & Profile page and a link to wishlists.html.

Initializes the Account & Profile page by fetching the current Profile, determining whether the user already has an account profile, and providing that data to the profile form and search-from-profile components.
Allows the user to create or update basic account details, including name, email, password, phone, and preferred destination. Handles validation and calls createOrUpdateProfile to persist changes.

Encapsulates the text input and autocomplete behavior for selecting or entering a preferred destination within the profile form, using getDestinationSuggestions to provide dynamic suggestions and maintaining both free-text and selected Destination.id states.

Provides quick navigation links from the Account & Profile page to related account areas, specifically wishlists.html and help.html. May use header summary data (e.g., defaultWishlistId) only for contextual display, not as navigation parameters.

A compact search helper that uses the user's profile preferences (particularly preferred destination) to pre-fill a rental search form. It collects destination, dates, and guest counts, calls startNewSearch to create a new SearchContext and initial results, and can navigate to search_results.html with the appropriate URL parameters.

Represents the global header search component as it behaves on the Account & Profile page. It allows the user to enter destination, dates, and guest counts, uses getDestinationSuggestions for destination autocomplete, and navigates to search_results.html with search parameters encoded in the URL when submitted.

Reads the source URL parameter on page load and adjusts initial page behavior, such as which section is focused or how the profile form is emphasized, without persisting this state beyond the current session or URL.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; card width suggests using 3–4 columns on desktop with large outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: full-width global header and nav (with the global search embedded), then a centered white main content area. Inside the main content, stack card-like sections vertically, aligned to the left and occupying roughly 35–40% of the content width on desktop. The right side of the content container remains mostly open, preserving the airy, left-biased composition from the mockup. The sequence within the content area is: header-user-summary at the top, then the profile-form card (with preferred-destination-autocomplete embedded inside it), followed by the account-links-panel card, and finally the search-from-profile card near the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: full-width global header and nav (with the global search embedded), then a centered white main content area. Inside the main content, stack card-like sections vertically, aligned to the left and occupying roughly 35–40% of the content width on desktop. The right side of the content container remains mostly open, preserving the airy, left-biased composition from the mockup. The sequence within the content area is: header-user-summary at the top, then the profile-form card (with preferred-destination-autocomplete embedded inside it), followed by the account-links-panel card, and finally the search-from-profile card near the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve the top ~10–12% of the viewport for the contact strip (not part of the provided components but implied by the mockup), and another ~15–20% for the logo + nav area that includes the global-header-search. The remaining ~70% of the viewport is the main content area. Within this content area, each main card (profile-form, account-links-panel, search-from-profile) spans about 35–40% of the central container width, left-aligned, with generous right-hand whitespace. Vertical spacing between major blocks (summary, cards) uses 32–48px (4–6 base units), while internal card padding uses 16–24px (2–3 base units).
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-information, editorial feel. Each card shows only essential elements with plenty of breathing room: large top margins, comfortable padding, minimal bordering, and soft shadows. Fields and links are not crowded; each label or control has sufficient vertical spacing (at least 8–16px) between them. The layout avoids multi-column form fields where possible, reinforcing the relaxed, lifestyle brand tone rather than a dense dashboard.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the strong vertical, slightly left-biased F-pattern from the mockup. The eye moves from the high-contrast top bar and navigation (with global search on the right) down to the left-aligned header-user-summary. From there, the focus naturally drops into the main profile-form card, then further down into the account-links-panel, and finally to the search-from-profile call to action. Within the profile-form, the preferred-destination-autocomplete is positioned so it becomes a focal field in the mid-upper section of the form. Loader and initializer behaviors appear centered over the main card area, briefly interrupting the flow only during data fetch and then disappearing.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a full-width, brand-green contact strip at the very top, followed by a white, airy navigation bar featuring the script logo on the left and a compact global search on the right. Below this fixed-width, centered header area, the main content sits in a wide container aligned to the center of the viewport but visually weighted to the left. Inside this container, a concise account header-summary block appears first, left-aligned and spanning about one-third of the page width, with generous whitespace above, to the right, and below it. Directly underneath, a large white card with soft rounded corners contains the profile form, again occupying roughly 35–40% of the content width while leaving the right side open and calm. Inside this card, fields are stacked vertically, with the preferred destination autocomplete running full width, visually highlighted among the other inputs. Beneath the form card, another slightly smaller card holds account-related links and navigation, mirroring the same width and alignment, creating a neat vertical stack of cards along the left. At the bottom of the content stack, a final slim card invites the user to start a new Hawaii rental search directly from their profile, maintaining the same left alignment and card styling. Throughout, vertical gaps of about 32–48px separate each section, and the spacious right-hand whitespace keeps the layout light, modern, and relaxed, echoing the resort-like tone of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Position the header-user-summary at the top of the main content area, directly below the global navigation bar. It should be left-aligned within the central page container and span roughly 35–40% of the container’s width (about 4 of 12 grid columns), echoing the card width used for property cards in the mockup. Vertically, place it with a clear margin of 32–48px below the navigation section, creating a strong visual break from the header. The summary itself can be a simple, low-chrome strip or mini-card with light padding (16–24px) and minimal border or shadow, containing the user's name and a short profile label (e.g., 'Traveler Profile') aligned to the left. The area to the right of this block remains mostly empty, reinforcing the left-biased composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable content; it scrolls away with the page while the global header remains fixed at the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-loader occupies the same horizontal footprint as the profile-form card. When active, it appears centered over the future form area, starting just below the header-user-summary. It should mimic the size and placement of the profile-form: left-aligned in the content container, spanning about 35–40% of the width, with a height similar to the expected form card. Use a light, subtle skeleton or shimmer card with rounded corners and internal placeholder lines that follow the same vertical stacking rhythm as the eventual fields. The loader should respect the standard vertical margins: 32–48px below the summary and with clear separation from any content that appears above or below it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlays or temporarily replaces the profile-form region; it is non-sticky and disappears entirely once data has loaded, yielding the same space to the profile-form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Place the profile-form as the dominant white card immediately below the header-user-summary (or the loader, if active), separated by a 32–48px vertical gap.
