The card is left-aligned, spanning roughly 35–40% of the content container width, with large outer gutters on the right to retain the airy, marketing-style composition. The card uses generous internal padding of 16–24px on all sides, with softly rounded corners and a subtle shadow, resembling a property card but with form fields instead of an image. Form fields are stacked vertically in a single column, each field occupying the full card width and separated by 12–16px vertical spacing. The top region may include a simple title like 'Account & Profile' and a short descriptive line, both aligned left. Primary actions (e.g., 'Save Changes') sit at the bottom of the card, aligned left, with ample space above 16–24px from the last field. The right side of the content area, next to the card, remains mostly empty to preserve the low-density feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card within the scroll; it naturally extends vertically as fields grow, but always maintains left alignment and consistent width relative to the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the profile-form card, position the preferred-destination-autocomplete as one of the key mid-upper fields, so it becomes a focal point when scanning the form. Horizontally, it should span the full inner width of the card, aligned flush with the form’s left and right padding. Vertically, place it after basic identity/contact fields (e.g., name, email) but before less critical preferences, separated by 12–16px from neighboring fields. The autocomplete dropdown opens downward inside the card’s bounds; where the suggestion list extends, it visually overlaps the card’s lower interior but stays within the same left alignment. Ensure there is at least 24–32px of free space below this field to accommodate the dropdown without feeling cramped. The styling mirrors other inputs but with slightly more vertical height for the field to hint at its interactive nature, while still respecting the airy spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard field within the form; its dropdown expands downward and may temporarily overlap following form fields, but does not change the card’s overall structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Place the account-links-panel as a separate card directly beneath the profile-form, maintaining the same left alignment and width (about 35–40% of the content container), so it visually stacks like another property card under the first. Leave a vertical gap of 32–40px between the bottom of the profile-form card and the top of this panel. The panel itself is a white, rounded card with subtle shadow and 16–24px internal padding. Inside, list navigation links (e.g., Wishlists, Bookings, Help) in a vertical column, each link given comfortable vertical spacing (8–12px) to avoid clutter. A simple heading like 'Account areas' may appear at the top, aligned left. The right-hand side of the page, alongside this card, remains mostly empty, reinforcing the open, editorial layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically stacked card; scrolls with the content and maintains consistent alignment and width relative to the profile-form card above it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Place the search-from-profile card below the account-links-panel as the last major block in the main content column. Maintain the same left alignment and width as the cards above, but this card can be slightly shorter in height, functioning more like a call-to-action strip than a full form. Leave a 32–40px vertical gap from the account-links-panel above. Inside, include a short title like 'Start a new Hawaii search' and a simplified search form (e.g., destination pre-filled from preferences, dates, and guests) arranged in a single horizontal row on larger screens when space allows, but still within the card’s width. On narrower viewports, the search fields stack vertically while preserving generous vertical spacing. The card retains 16–24px internal padding and a subtle shadow, echoing the card language of the rest of the page. It sits above the page footer with an additional 40–64px of bottom margin for breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static call-to-action card; no sticky behavior, but its compact form and position at the end of the content flow make it a natural next step after adjusting profile settings.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Embed the global-header-search into the primary navigation bar near the top of the page. Horizontally, position it on the right side of the nav, opposite the logo and main navigation links which sit on the left. The search field should be relatively compact, spanning roughly 20–30% of the nav bar width on desktop, with ample horizontal padding from the right edge to avoid feeling cramped. Vertically, it is centered within the nav bar’s height, aligning with the baseline of the nav links. Above the nav is the brand-green contact strip; below it is the main content area. The search maintains the minimal, clean styling of the overall UI, with a subtle outline and soft rounded corners, and does not dominate the header but is clearly visible as a utility element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the global header section; if the header itself is sticky, the global-header-search remains visible at the top as the user scrolls the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The source-based-initializer is a non-visual controller component that influences initial state (e.g., pre-filling fields or selecting tabs) rather than occupying a distinct visual region. Conceptually, it is tied to the same region as the profile-form and account area content. Its ‘placement’ can be thought of as logically attached to the main content column, initializing values before the header-user-summary and profile-form render. It does not produce a visible element, card, or spacing, and therefore does not alter the vertical rhythm, margins, or widths defined for the other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; runs behind the scenes without affecting layout, spacing, or scroll behavior.

name: Wishlists Overview
title: Wishlists
description: Page that lists all user wishlists, lets users create, rename, and delete wishlists, and allows navigation to each wishlist’s detailed view while keeping global search available.

core_features: Load and display all wishlists for the current user using getWishlistsOverview, including each wishlist’s name, optional description, cover image reference, rental_count, is_default status, and timestamps.
Use getHeaderUserSummary to show basic account context in the header, including whether a profile exists, the profile name if available, and the default wishlist’s rental_count.
Enable creation of a new wishlist from the overview page via createWishlist with user-provided name, optional description, and an option to mark it as default.
Allow users to rename existing wishlists by submitting a new name through renameWishlist and updating the corresponding wishlist in the overview list.
Allow users to delete existing wishlists (and their items) via deleteWishlist, then update the overview list to reflect the removal.
Enable navigation into an individual wishlist detail view by clicking a wishlist entry, which navigates to wishlist.html with wishlistId set to the selected wishlist’s id.
Provide destination autocomplete in the global header search using getDestinationSuggestions, based on user-entered query text.
Start a new rental search from the header via startNewSearch, then navigate to search_results.html with URL parameters derived from the search context and header form values.
Use the optional highlightWishlistId URL parameter to highlight a specific wishlist in the overview, typically after interactions on other pages that create or modify a wishlist.
user_workflows: Viewing existing wishlists: 1) User navigates to wishlists.html (optionally with highlightWishlistId in the URL). 2) On load, the page reads highlightWishlistId from the URL if present. 3) The page calls getHeaderUserSummary to populate header context (profile name and default wishlist info). 4) The page calls getWishlistsOverview to retrieve all wishlists. 5) The overview list component renders each wishlist as an interactive entry. 6) If highlightWishlistId matches one of the returned wishlists, that entry is marked as highlighted in the page state.

Creating a new wishlist from the overview: 1) User activates the create-wishlist action (for example, a button that opens a simple form or inline input). 2) User enters a wishlist name and optionally a description, and optionally selects whether to make it the default wishlist. 3) User submits the form, triggering a call to createWishlist with the entered name, description, and makeDefault flag. 4) On a successful response, the new Wishlist object is added to the in-page wishlist collection. 5) The page updates the URL using the History API to set highlightWishlistId to the new wishlist’s id (without reloading), and sets the highlighted wishlist state accordingly. 6) The new wishlist entry appears in the list and is highlighted; if makeDefault was true, header summary can be refreshed on demand by re-calling getHeaderUserSummary or by updating the cached default counts if available in the response.

Renaming an existing wishlist: 1) User activates a rename action on a specific wishlist entry (for example, via a contextual control on that entry). 2) The entry switches into an editable state with a name input populated from the current wishlist.name. 3) User modifies the name and confirms the change, triggering a call to renameWishlist with wishlistId set to the selected wishlist’s id and newName set to the user-entered value. 4) On success (success = true), the component replaces the old wishlist.name with the returned wishlist.name from the response and exits edit mode. 5) If success = false, the component shows the returned message and keeps the previous name in the UI until the user retries or cancels.

Deleting a wishlist: 1) User activates a delete action for a specific wishlist entry. 2) The page shows a confirmation interaction (for example, a dialog or inline confirmation state). 3) If the user confirms, the page calls deleteWishlist with wishlistId equal to the selected wishlist’s id. 4) On success (success = true), the wishlist is removed from the in-page wishlist collection and the list rerenders without that entry. 5) If the deleted wishlist id equals the current highlightWishlistId in the URL, the page updates the URL (via History API) to remove highlightWishlistId and clears highlight state. 6) If success = false, the component displays the returned message and keeps the wishlist in the list.

Opening a wishlist detail view: 1) User clicks on a wishlist entry that is not in an edit or delete-confirmation state. 2) The page reads that entry’s id and constructs a navigation to wishlist.html with the wishlistId query parameter set to this id (e.g., wishlist.html?wishlistId=<id>). 3) Browser navigates to wishlist.html where the wishlist detail page uses the provided wishlistId for its own data loading.

Starting a new search from the header while on the wishlists page: 1) In the header search form, user begins typing a destination. 2) On text input, the header component calls getDestinationSuggestions with query set to the current text. 3) The component presents the list of Destination suggestions; user selects a destination, which sets an internal headerSearchDestination object (containing id and name). 4) User selects or inputs check-in and check-out dates and chooses adults, children, and pets counts. 5) When user submits the header search, the component calls startNewSearch with destinationId = headerSearchDestination.id, checkIn/checkOut as ISO 8601 strings derived from the date inputs, and adultsCount/childrenCount/petsCount derived from the corresponding controls. 6) On success, the component takes the returned searchContext and navigates to search_results.html using URL parameters destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and pets mapped from searchContext and/or the submitted form values.
interactions: On page load, the page-controller component reads the highlightWishlistId URL parameter (if present) and uses getWishlistsOverview to obtain the current list of wishlists; it then sets a highlighted flag for the matching wishlist in the local state.

When the user opens the create-wishlist form and submits it, an onSubmit handler calls createWishlist; if successful, the page adds the returned wishlist to the list, updates the URL’s highlightWishlistId to the new id, and sets the corresponding entry as highlighted.

When the user activates rename on a specific wishlist, an onClick handler toggles that wishlist card into edit mode, displaying a text input bound to the current wishlist name; on submission, an onSubmit handler calls renameWishlist and updates the wishlist entry on success.

When the user initiates a delete action on a wishlist, an onClick handler opens a confirmation interaction; if confirmed, an onConfirm handler calls deleteWishlist and, on successful response, removes the wishlist from the list and clears highlightWishlistId if it referred to the deleted wishlist.
When the user clicks on a wishlist card outside of rename/delete controls, an onClick handler navigates to wishlist.html with a wishlistId query parameter set to that wishlist’s id.

In the header search field, onInput handlers call getDestinationSuggestions with the current query string to populate a suggestions list; selection of a suggestion sets an internal headerSearchDestination state used for subsequent search submission.

When the user submits the header search form, an onSubmit handler calls startNewSearch using the destination, dates, and guest counts from the header state; after a successful response, the handler constructs a URL to search_results.html with appropriate query parameters and navigates there.

When the user navigates back to wishlists.html from other pages (for example, via header or footer navigation), the page re-executes getHeaderUserSummary and getWishlistsOverview to ensure the list reflects any wishlist changes made elsewhere, again honoring any highlightWishlistId present in the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for the page is controlled via URL query parameters. The page reads the highlightWishlistId parameter from the wishlists.html URL on initial load and whenever the URL changes (e.g., via browser navigation). If highlightWishlistId is present and matches an id returned by getWishlistsOverview, the corresponding wishlist entry is marked as highlighted in the page’s internal state, which may be used for visual emphasis or automatic scrolling behavior. If highlightWishlistId is absent or does not match any returned wishlist, no entry is highlighted. When a new wishlist is created via createWishlist, the page updates the current URL with highlightWishlistId set to the new wishlist id using the History API (pushState or replaceState) without adding any data that does not come from the API; internal state is updated to align with this parameter. When a wishlist is deleted via deleteWishlist and its id equals the current highlightWishlistId, the page updates the URL to remove highlightWishlistId and simultaneously clears any highlight flags in the internal state. All other interactive state, such as which wishlist is currently being renamed, whether a delete confirmation is open, the current header search input text, selected destination suggestion, and selected dates and guest counts, is maintained as in-memory component state only and is not persisted in URL parameters or localStorage. Navigation to wishlist.html passes wishlistId via the URL, and navigation to search_results.html passes destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and pets as URL parameters derived from the latest header search submission and/or the returned SearchContext object from startNewSearch.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading and high-level state for wishlists.html. On initialization, it parses the highlightWishlistId URL parameter, calls getHeaderUserSummary and getWishlistsOverview, stores the returned data in memory, and determines which wishlist (if any) should be marked as highlighted. It exposes callbacks for creating, renaming, and deleting wishlists, and for updating the URL when highlightWishlistId changes.
event_handlers: onLoad: parse URL query parameters (including highlightWishlistId) and invoke getHeaderUserSummary and getWishlistsOverview; set initial highlightedWishlistId state based on the URL and fetched wishlists.
onWishlistCreated: receive new Wishlist from createWishlist, append it to the list, set highlightedWishlistId to the new id, and update the URL (via History API) to include highlightWishlistId.
onWishlistRenamed: receive updated Wishlist from renameWishlist and replace the corresponding entry in the wishlist collection while preserving highlight state.

onWishlistDeleted: after a successful deleteWishlist call, remove the wishlist from the list; if the deleted id equals the current highlightedWishlistId, clear highlight in internal state and remove highlightWishlistId from the URL using History API.

onUrlChange: when the browser URL changes (e.g., back/forward navigation), re-parse highlightWishlistId and update the highlightedWishlistId state; if necessary, reconcile with the existing wishlist collection.
onRefreshHeaderSummary: optionally re-call getHeaderUserSummary when global wishlist state changes (such as when a wishlist is created with makeDefault true) to keep header indicators accurate.

components.functionality: Renders the global header context for wishlists.html, including profile summary, an indicator of the default wishlist rental_count, navigation links (including back to the wishlists overview itself if other pages reuse this header), and a global search form that supports destination suggestions and new search initiation.
event_handlers: onLoad: call getHeaderUserSummary to determine hasProfile, profileName, defaultWishlistId, and defaultWishlistRentalCount, then display corresponding information in the header.

onSearchDestinationInput: on each change to the destination text input, call getDestinationSuggestions with query equal to the current text and show a suggestions list bound to the returned Destination objects.
onSearchDestinationSelect: when a suggestion is selected, store its id and name in headerSearchDestination state to be used on search submission.

onSearchFormSubmit: prevent default form submission, then call startNewSearch with destinationId from headerSearchDestination.id, checkIn/checkOut converted to ISO 8601 from the header date controls, and adultsCount/childrenCount/petsCount derived from the header guest controls; on success, read the returned SearchContext and navigate to search_results.html with destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and pets URL parameters based on this SearchContext and/or the submitted values.

onClickWishlistsNav: when the user clicks the Wishlists navigation link in the header (if present), navigate to wishlists.html, optionally preserving or clearing existing query parameters according to the site’s navigation rules.

components.functionality: Displays the collection of wishlists returned by getWishlistsOverview. It receives the current highlightedWishlistId from the page controller, marks the matching wishlist entry as highlighted, and renders child wishlist-card components for each Wishlist entity.
event_handlers: onWishlistCardClick: when a non-editing wishlist-card is clicked, call a navigation handler to go to wishlist.html with wishlistId set to that card’s wishlist.id.

onStartRename: triggered when a rename action is requested on a particular wishlist-card; sets that card’s editing state in the list controller so only one wishlist is in rename mode at a time if desired.
onStartDelete: triggered when a delete action is requested; passes the target wishlist.id to a delete-confirmation component or handler.
onHighlightChange: listens for changes in highlightedWishlistId from the page controller and updates the internal highlight state for each wishlist-card.

components.functionality: Represents a single wishlist entry within the list. It displays the wishlist’s name, optional description, rental_count, cover_image reference, and whether it is the default wishlist. It also exposes controls for rename and delete actions and can enter an inline edit state for renaming.
event_handlers: onClick: if the card is not in edit or delete-confirmation mode, navigate to wishlist.html with a wishlistId query parameter equal to the bound wishlist.id.
onClickRename: prevent navigation and emit an event to the parent list to enter rename mode for this wishlist, showing the wishlist-rename-form for this card.
onClickDelete: prevent navigation and emit an event to open a delete confirmation for this wishlist (handled by the wishlist-delete-confirmation component or list controller).
onHighlightStateChange: respond to a change in a highlighted flag (provided by the parent list) to adjust the card’s state (for example, to indicate that this entry matches highlightWishlistId).

components.functionality: Provides user inputs to create a new wishlist from the overview page. Accepts a name, optional description, and an option to mark the new wishlist as the default. On submission, it invokes createWishlist and hands the resulting Wishlist back to the page controller.
event_handlers: onOpenForm: triggered when the user activates the create-wishlist action; resets local input state and displays the form.
onNameChange: update local state for the wishlist name as the user types.
onDescriptionChange: update local state for the optional description as the user types.
onMakeDefaultToggle: update local state for the makeDefault flag when the user toggles it.

onSubmit: validate required fields (at minimum, that a non-empty name exists), then call createWishlist with name, description, and makeDefault derived from the local state; on success, emit an onWishlistCreated event to the page controller with the returned Wishlist object and reset/close the form; on failure, display the error message if available without introducing any hardcoded fallback content.

components.functionality: Allows the user to change the name of an existing wishlist directly within its card. It captures the new name and calls renameWishlist, then updates the parent list with the returned Wishlist.
event_handlers: onEnterRenameMode: initializes the input field with the existing wishlist.name for the selected wishlist card.
onNameInputChange: updates local rename input state as the user types.

onSubmit: call renameWishlist with wishlistId equal to the current wishlist.id and newName equal to the input state; on success (success = true), emit an onWishlistRenamed event with the returned Wishlist to the page controller and exit rename mode; on failure (success = false), display the returned message and keep the user in rename mode until they correct the issue or cancel.
onCancel: exit rename mode, discard local input changes, and restore the display of the original wishlist name.

components.functionality: Manages the confirmation workflow for deleting a wishlist. It is invoked from a specific wishlist-card, calls deleteWishlist if the user confirms, and informs the page controller of the result.
event_handlers: onOpen: receive the target wishlist.id and name from the parent component and show the confirmation state without hardcoding any labels derived from static data.

onConfirmDelete: call deleteWishlist with wishlistId equal to the target wishlist.id; on success (success = true), emit an onWishlistDeleted event to the page controller so the list can be updated; on failure (success = false), show the returned message and keep the dialog open or close it based on configuration.
onCancelDelete: close the confirmation dialog without calling deleteWishlist and without altering the wishlist list.

components.functionality: Displays an empty-state message and a prompt to create a first wishlist when getWishlistsOverview returns an empty collection. The content should be dynamic or generic, not tied to any specific property names or counts beyond what the API provides.
event_handlers: onShow: triggered when the wishlist-overview-page-controller detects that there are no wishlists in the getWishlistsOverview response; the panel becomes visible.
onClickCreateFirstWishlist: triggers the opening of the wishlist-create-form when the user wants to create a first wishlist.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; card width suggests using 3–4 columns on desktop with large outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps a linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width contact strip at the very top, followed by a full-width logo + navigation header, then a centered main content area. Inside the content area, wishlist controls (title, create form, view toggle if present) are aligned to the left, with the wishlist cards stacked vertically beneath them. The card column sits in the left 30–40% of the content width, leaving generous white space to the right to preserve the airy, editorial feel and to allow for more cards at other breakpoints.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps a linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width contact strip at the very top, followed by a full-width logo + navigation header, then a centered main content area. Inside the content area, wishlist controls (title, create form, view toggle if present) are aligned to the left, with the wishlist cards stacked vertically beneath them. The card column sits in the left 30–40% of the content width, leaving generous white space to the right to preserve the airy, editorial feel and to allow for more cards at other breakpoints.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The contact bar is a shallow, dense band (about 10–12% of the overall header height), spanning full width in brand green. Below it, the main navigation/header occupies an additional 15–20% of the initial viewport height, with logo, navigation, and global search. The remainder of the viewport is devoted to the main content area: a centered container on a mostly white background. Within the content area, the left column for wishlist controls and cards takes roughly 35–40% of the container width on desktop, with large outer gutters and an open right side. Vertical spacing between header and content, controls and list, and between cards follows the 32–48px section margin rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally sparse and relaxed: few interactive elements visible at once, large paddings inside cards (16–24px), and generous margins between major sections. Each wishlist card has a large, image-dominant thumbnail to echo the vacation lifestyle feel, with minimal supporting text. Forms (create, rename) are visually light and integrated into the cards rather than heavy standalone modules. The overall density remains low, prioritizing a calm, aspirational mood over data-heavy efficiency.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is pulled first to the high-contrast green contact strip at the top, then moves down to the white header with logo and navigation, where the active 'Wishlists' (or equivalent) nav item with a green underline anchors the user's context. From there, attention moves to the main content: a left-aligned page title and controls, then straight down the vertical stack of wishlist cards. Within each card, the image at the top captures attention, followed by the wishlist name and key actions below. The flow respects a top-to-bottom, slightly left-biased F-pattern, with controls and primary information starting at the left and cascading downward.

layout.overall_description: The Wishlists Overview page sits on a clean, mostly white canvas with a relaxed, resort-inspired tone. At the very top, a slim full-width green contact bar stretches edge-to-edge, tightly packed with contact details. Immediately below, a taller white header band contains the script logo with palm icon on the left, horizontal navigation links, and a compact global search on the right, all sitting within a centered content container and framed by generous left and right gutters. The active navigation item for the wishlist area is underlined in brand green. Beneath the header, the main content area begins after a comfortable vertical gap, still centered in the viewport. On desktop, the content is clearly left-weighted: along the left 35–40% of the container, the page title and wishlist create control appear at the top, followed by a vertical column of wishlist cards that run downward, each card featuring a wide photo on top and text below. The right side of the content area remains mostly empty white space, reinforcing the airy, lifestyle feel and allowing the cards to stand out like editorial tiles. When there are no wishlists, a single, friendly empty-state panel occupies that left column, encouraging the user to create their first wishlist. Rename happens inline within a card, and deletion triggers a centered modal overlay, but in all cases the layout stays quiet, with soft shadows, rounded corners, and the brand green used sparingly for emphasis.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines a full-width canvas with a soft off-white or very light gray background and centers the main content within a max-width container (approximately 1120–1200px) aligned to the viewport center. It establishes a 12-column responsive grid where the main wishlist column consumes roughly 4 columns (around 35–40% of the container width) on desktop, starting just inside the left gutter, and leaves the remaining columns to the right mostly empty. The controller ensures the header (contact bar + navigation) stays at the very top, with the main content area starting below after a vertical gap of about 32–48px. All vertical spacing between sections (header to content, controls to list, list to footer) uses multiples of the 8px base unit, with larger breaks of 32–48px to keep the layout airy. On scroll, the header scrolls away with the content (no fixed positioning by default), preserving a simple, vertical document flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines the overall grid and centered content container; standard document flow with responsive behavior but no fixed or sticky positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-bar spans the full browser width at the top of the page and is composed of two vertically stacked bands. The upper band is a shallow contact strip, about 40–48px tall, with a solid brand green background and white text. It stretches edge-to-edge with no side padding break, but its content (phone, email, possibly a small icon row) is aligned within the same centered max-width container as the rest of the site, left-justified to echo the mockup’s pattern of dense information in a compact strip. Directly beneath it sits the main navigation/header section, taller (roughly 80–120px on desktop), with a white background and a very subtle bottom shadow or divider line. Within this band, the logo in script with a palm icon is anchored at the far left of the content container, navigation links sit horizontally to the right of the logo, and a compact, right-aligned global search field occupies the far right, visually balanced but not dominating. The active section for the wishlist area is indicated with a brand green underline and possibly a slight color change, guiding the eye downward into the page. A comfortable 24–32px of space separates the bottom of this nav/header band from the start of the main content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the top of the document flow, full-width. By default it scrolls away with content (non-sticky), maintaining the simple vertical flow from the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-list component occupies a left-aligned column within the centered content container, immediately below the page-level wishlist controls (title and create form), starting after a vertical gap of about 24–32px. On desktop, its width is constrained to roughly 35–40% of the overall content container, creating a tall, narrow column that echoes the single-card mockup state and leaves expansive white space on the right. Within this column, wishlist cards are stacked vertically, one per row, with consistent gaps of approximately 32px between them. The list aligns its left edge with the left edge of the page title and create form, reinforcing a clean vertical line. When enough cards are present, the list scrolls vertically with the page; there is no internal scrolling or grid packing, keeping the layout simple and editorial rather than dense. On narrower viewports (tablet/mobile), the list can expand closer to full width, but always preserves comfortable side margins and vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows vertically within the main content column; standard document flow with responsive width adjustments, no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each wishlist-card is a white, rounded rectangle with a soft shadow, occupying the full width of the wishlist-list column (roughly 35–40% of the content container on desktop). The card height is substantial because it is image-first: the top half to two-thirds of the card is dominated by a wide photo thumbnail (e.g., a representative property or collage) that spans edge-to-edge horizontally within the card and maintains a cinematic aspect ratio (around 16:9 or 3:2). The image has rounded top corners that match the card radius. Below the image, a text block with 16–24px padding on all sides contains the wishlist name as a primary line of text aligned to the top left of the block, with a secondary line beneath for metadata (such as number of stays, last updated) in a lighter tone. Primary actions like "View wishlist" or subtle icons for rename/delete are aligned to the right side of this text block, maintaining a clean separation between title and controls. Vertical spacing inside the text block follows the 8px base unit (e.g., 8–16px between title and metadata). The bottom corners are rounded, and the card sits slightly lifted from the background via a soft shadow, with at least 24–32px of whitespace separating it from other cards above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level card within the wishlist-list column; expands to full column width and height determined by its content and fixed image aspect ratio.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-create-form appears at the top of the main content area, left-aligned within the same column as the wishlist-list. It sits directly beneath the page heading (e.g., "Your wishlists") with a vertical gap of about 16–24px, and directly above the first wishlist card. In its default, collapsed state, it may present as a subtle, pill-shaped "Create new wishlist" button with a plus icon, aligned to the far left and leaving ample whitespace to the right. When expanded, the form takes on a card-like appearance consistent with the design language: a low-height, white panel with rounded corners and light shadow, matching the width of the wishlist column. Inside, a single-line text input stretches across approximately 70–80% of the card’s width, aligned left, with a primary submit button (e.g., in brand green) positioned to the right of the input, maintaining a horizontal layout. Internal padding of 16–24px around the content keeps the form feeling spacious. There is clear vertical separation (24–32px) between this form card and the first wishlist card below it, preserving the airy feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element at the top of the wishlist column; appears in the normal document flow and expands/collapses in place without overlapping other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-rename-form is an inline, transient overlay within an existing wishlist-card, replacing just the title row area in the card’s text block. When activated, the static title text disappears and is replaced by a single-line text input that spans roughly 70–80% of the available text block width, left-aligned, maintaining the same left padding as the original title for alignment. On the right side of this row, lightweight confirmation and cancel controls (icons or compact buttons) sit inline, aligned to the card’s right padding edge, so the entire rename interaction remains on a single horizontal line. Vertical padding above and below the input matches the original title’s spacing to avoid changing the overall card height more than necessary. The rest of the card (image, metadata, other actions) remains visible and unchanged, reinforcing that this is a small, focused edit state within the card rather than a modal or separate panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline within a specific wishlist-card, occupying the title area in the normal document flow without shifting the card out of place or overlaying neighboring cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-delete-confirmation displays as a centered modal dialog overlaying the entire page when triggered. A semi-transparent dark scrim covers the full viewport, dimming the background but leaving the header and content faintly visible to maintain context. The dialog itself is a white card with rounded corners and a soft shadow, visually consistent with other cards but more compact and focused. It is horizontally centered and vertically positioned slightly above the exact midpoint (for a more natural visual balance), with a width of approximately 30–35% of the viewport on desktop (around 400–480px) and responsive narrowing on smaller screens. Inside, there is generous padding of 24–32px. The confirmation message is top-aligned, left-justified, with the wishlist name emphasized. Below, a row of actions is aligned to the right: a more prominent destructive button in brand or accent color and a secondary cancel action. The modal leaves ample whitespace around its content and between text and buttons, reinforcing the calm, deliberate nature of the confirmation step.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position modal centered in the viewport with a full-screen scrim; overlays all other components and prevents interaction with the underlying page until dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-empty-state replaces the wishlist-list within the left column when the user has no wishlists. It appears in the same position where the first card would normally be, directly below the page title and create control, preserving the left-aligned flow. The panel is a larger, inviting card occupying the full width of the wishlist column (about 35–40% of the content container on desktop) with rounded corners, a soft shadow, and generous internal padding of 24–32px. The content is vertically stacked: an illustrative icon or subtle graphic at the top (optional), a bold heading such as "You don’t have any wishlists yet" beneath it, then a short, friendly explanatory paragraph, and finally a primary call-to-action button (e.g., "Create your first wishlist") aligned left to echo the rest of the page. Ample vertical spacing (16–24px) separates each text element. Surrounding the panel is open white space, especially to the right and below, reinforcing the spacious, lifestyle feel and drawing the eye naturally to the next step.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline in the main content column in place of the wishlist-list; standard document flow, no overlay, and it collapses away when wishlists exist.

name: Wishlist Detail
title: Wishlist Detail
description: Displays rentals saved in a specific wishlist and lets users review listings, manage saved rentals, and start new searches using the wishlist as a template.

core_features: Load wishlist details for the wishlistId from the URL and retrieve all associated rentals using getWishlistDetails.
Display all wishlist items as rental cards with key information from RentalProperty and allow navigation to full listing pages (listing.html?rentalId=...).
Allow users to remove individual rentals from the wishlist using removeWishlistItem, with the UI updating counts and the visible list.
Use getHeaderUserSummary to show basic user and default wishlist information in the header (e.g., profile name and count of saved rentals).

Provide a search-from-wishlist feature that can derive a destination template from the wishlist, optionally accept an override via destination autocomplete using getDestinationSuggestions, and initiate a new search with startNewSearch.
Allow users to save rentals returned from a wishlist-inspired search into the currently viewed wishlist using saveRentalToWishlist.
Support navigation to search_results.html with URL parameters derived from the wishlist and/or the SearchContext returned by startNewSearch.
Handle empty-wishlist cases by showing an appropriate state and exposing search entry points that use the wishlist context.
user_workflows: View a wishlist: 1) User navigates from wishlists.html to wishlist.html with wishlistId in the URL. 2) On page load, the header component calls getHeaderUserSummary. 3) The wishlist loader component reads wishlistId from the URL and calls getWishlistDetails(wishlistId). 4) On success, the wishlist summary and wishlist rental list components render the wishlist information and rental cards. 5) If getWishlistDetails fails or returns no items, an error or empty state is shown instead of the rental list.

Open a full rental listing from the wishlist: 1) User scans rental cards in the wishlist rental list. 2) User clicks on a rental card or a dedicated “View listing” action. 3) The card component reads rental.id from the bound RentalProperty and navigates to listing.html with rentalId set to that value in the URL query string. 4) The wishlist page remains unchanged; the listing page takes over to handle booking actions.

Remove a rental from the wishlist: 1) User clicks a remove action for a specific rental card. 2) The card or a confirmation modal triggers removeWishlistItem with the wishlistItemId for that item. 3) While awaiting the response, the UI can show a pending state for that item. 4) On success (success=true), the wishlist loader or list component removes the corresponding item from local state, updates any displayed rental_count, and re-renders the list. 5) Optionally, an undo action can re-add the rental by calling saveRentalToWishlist with the current wishlistId and the rentalPropertyId associated with the removed item.

Start a search inspired by the wishlist (basic template): 1) User clicks a “Search similar stays” entry point (from the wishlist summary bar or empty state). 2) The search-from-wishlist component derives a destination template from the wishlist (for example, based on the destination_id values of the rentals) and optionally reads any existing template parameters (destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets) from the wishlist.html URL. 3) If the component has a complete set of required parameters for startNewSearch (destinationId, checkIn, checkOut, adultsCount, childrenCount, petsCount), it calls startNewSearch with those values. 4) On success, the component receives a SearchContext and an array of RentalProperty results. 5) The component stores this data in memory and can show a preview list of results. 6) When the user chooses to view all results, the component navigates to search_results.html, populating the URL parameters (destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets) using values from the SearchContext.

Override the search destination using autocomplete: 1) User opens the search-from-wishlist panel or section. 2) User starts typing into the destination input field. 3) On each change (after debouncing), the destination autocomplete component calls getDestinationSuggestions with the current input text as query. 4) The component displays the returned Destination suggestions. 5) When the user selects a suggestion, the component stores the chosen destination.id and destination.name as the template destination. 6) The search-from-wishlist component updates its derived template and, when the user submits the search, uses this destinationId and name in the startNewSearch call and in any subsequent navigation to search_results.html.

Save search results into the wishlist: 1) After starting a wishlist-inspired search, the search-from-wishlist component receives RentalProperty results from startNewSearch. 2) The search results preview list renders each result as a card, showing basic rental details. 3) For a result the user wants to keep, the user clicks a “Save to this wishlist” action on that card. 4) The component calls saveRentalToWishlist with rentalPropertyId from the selected result and wishlistId from the current wishlist. 5) On success, the component can either append the newly added wishlist item into the existing wishlist state (if the page keeps the wishlist open) or update an indicator that the rental is now saved in this wishlist.

Handle empty wishlists and encourage search: 1) If getWishlistDetails returns a wishlist with no items, the wishlist rental list detects the empty state. 2) Instead of cards, the empty state component renders, including a control to start a search from this wishlist. 3) When clicked, the search-from-wishlist component follows the same flow as the standard search-from-wishlist workflow, using any template data from the URL or from derived destination information.
interactions: On page load, a global initialization interaction reads wishlistId and any optional template search parameters from the URL query string and triggers data loading via getHeaderUserSummary and getWishlistDetails.
Clicking on a rental card or its “View listing” action triggers navigation to listing.html with rentalId set based on the associated RentalProperty.id.

Clicking a remove action on a rental triggers a confirmation interaction (if enabled) and then calls removeWishlistItem with the relevant wishlistItemId; success or failure responses adjust UI state and error messaging.
Clicking an undo removal action (when available) calls saveRentalToWishlist with the removed rental’s rentalPropertyId and the current wishlistId to restore the item.

Toggling sort options or view modes for the wishlist rental list updates the query parameters in the wishlist.html URL (for example, sort or viewMode), reorders the in-memory list accordingly, and maintains that order on refresh or direct linking.

Typing into the destination input within the search-from-wishlist component triggers a debounced call to getDestinationSuggestions, and selecting a suggestion updates the internal template destinationId and destinationName, and can also update corresponding URL parameters.

Clicking the primary search submission control in the search-from-wishlist component validates that all required fields for startNewSearch are available, calls startNewSearch, stores the returned SearchContext and results, and exposes a control to navigate to search_results.html with URL parameters derived from that SearchContext.

Clicking “View all results” in the search-from-wishlist preview navigates the user to search_results.html and sets the URL parameters destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and pets according to the current search template or SearchContext.

Clicking “Save to this wishlist” on any search result card in the preview triggers saveRentalToWishlist and, on success, visually marks the rental as saved and optionally updates the wishlist rental list if it is shown on the same page.
Clicking a link or button to return to the wishlists overview navigates to wishlists.html without modifying any wishlist data.
page_functionality.state_logic: The main persistent page state is controlled through URL parameters and API responses. The wishlistId URL parameter is required; if it is missing, the page cannot load a wishlist and should show an error or redirect. On initial load or when wishlistId changes in the URL, the wishlist loader component calls getWishlistDetails(wishlistId) and stores the returned Wishlist and items in memory. Optional URL parameters can control list behavior, such as sort (e.g., addedAt, price, rating) and viewMode (e.g., list, grid). The wishlist rental list component reads these parameters to determine how to order and present items and updates the URL when the user changes these options so the state is shareable and persists on reload. For search templating, the URL for wishlist.html may optionally include destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and pets. When present, the search-from-wishlist component uses these as the initial template for both startNewSearch calls and for navigation to search_results.html. If only partial information is available, the component can still allow navigation that only uses known destination parameters while disabling or skipping startNewSearch, which requires all of its parameters. When startNewSearch is called, its returned SearchContext and results are kept in in-memory component state (not in URL or localStorage) for displaying preview results. Any navigation to search_results.html uses the SearchContext fields (destination_id, destination_name, check_in, check_out, adults_count, children_count, pets_count) mapped directly into URL query parameters (destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, pets) so that the search results page can reconstruct or continue the search. Header state derived from getHeaderUserSummary is not persisted in the URL but is refreshed on each page load. No wishlist or search state is stored in localStorage; persistence across loads relies on URL parameters and fresh API calls.

components.functionality: Loads and displays user summary information in the site header, including whether a profile exists, the profile name, and default wishlist indicators. Provides navigation links to profile and wishlists pages without altering wishlist details.
event_handlers: onLoad: Call getHeaderUserSummary with no parameters when the page initializes and bind hasProfile, profileName, defaultWishlistId, and defaultWishlistRentalCount into header state.
onClickProfile: Navigate to the account/profile page using a standard URL without modifying data.
onClickDefaultWishlistLink: Navigate to wishlist.html with wishlistId set to defaultWishlistId, replacing the current wishlist context.

components.functionality: Controls retrieval of the wishlist and its items based on the wishlistId URL parameter. Handles loading, success, and error states and passes data to dependent components such as the summary bar and rental list.
event_handlers: onLoad: Read wishlistId from the wishlist.html URL query string; if present, call getWishlistDetails(wishlistId).
onParamChange: When wishlistId changes in the URL (for example through navigation from another wishlist), re-call getWishlistDetails with the new wishlistId.
onLoadSuccess: Store the returned wishlist and items array in in-memory state and notify child components to render.
onLoadError: Store error state and notify child components to show an error message instead of the rental list.
onItemsUpdated: When a child component removes or re-adds a wishlist item, update the in-memory items collection and any derived counts and propagate changes to the summary bar.

components.functionality: Displays high-level information about the current wishlist such as its name, description (if available), and number of saved rentals. Provides actions to navigate back to the wishlists overview and to initiate a search based on this wishlist.
event_handlers: onWishlistDataChanged: When the wishlist-loader provides or updates Wishlist data, display its name, optional description, and rental_count or the computed length of items.
onClickBackToWishlists: Navigate the user to wishlists.html via URL navigation without modifying wishlist data.
onClickSearchSimilar: Trigger opening of the wishlist-derived-search component panel or section, passing the current Wishlist object and any relevant URL parameters so it can build a search template.

components.functionality: Renders a collection of wishlist items as rental cards, manages sorting and view modes, and handles empty-list behavior by delegating to an empty-state component when there are no items.
event_handlers: onItemsLoaded: Receive the items array from wishlist-loader, apply current sort and viewMode rules derived from URL parameters, and render a wishlist-item-card for each entry.
onChangeSort: When a user selects a different sort option, update the sort parameter in the URL and re-order the in-memory items without additional API calls.
onChangeViewMode: When a user toggles between allowed view modes, update the viewMode parameter in the URL and re-render the layout.

onItemRemoved: When a wishlist-item-card notifies that an item has been successfully removed, remove it from the items collection; if the list becomes empty, render the wishlist-empty-state component instead.

onItemReadded: When a wishlist-item-card or search preview indicates that an item has been added back into the current wishlist, append or update the item in the items collection and refresh the rendered cards.

components.functionality: Represents a single saved rental within the wishlist, showing key fields from the associated RentalProperty and controls for viewing the full listing and managing its presence in the wishlist.
event_handlers: onClickOpenListing: Use the bound RentalProperty.id as rentalId to navigate to listing.html with rentalId set in the URL; no API call is required for this navigation.

onClickRemove: Optionally open the removal-confirmation-modal; on confirmation, call removeWishlistItem with the WishlistItem.id and, on success, emit an onItemRemoved event to the wishlist-rental-list and wishlist-loader components.
onRemoveSuccess: Temporarily update local state to hide the card and optionally expose an undo control.

onClickUndoRemove: If an undo is available, call saveRentalToWishlist with rentalPropertyId from the associated RentalProperty and the current wishlistId; on success, emit an onItemReadded event so parent components can restore the card.
onError: If removeWishlistItem or saveRentalToWishlist returns success=false, display an error state for this card and leave its presence in the list unchanged.

components.functionality: Optional confirmation dialog that appears before permanently removing a rental from the wishlist. Confirms the user intent and then delegates to the removal logic in the card component.
event_handlers: onOpen: Receive context for the targeted WishlistItem and RentalProperty from the invoking wishlist-item-card and display confirmation content.
onConfirmRemove: Call removeWishlistItem with the provided wishlistItemId; on success, close the modal and trigger the card’s onRemoveSuccess and the list’s onItemRemoved handlers.
onCancelRemove: Close the modal without calling removeWishlistItem and without changing the list state.

components.functionality: Handles the case where the wishlist has no items. Displays a message indicating that there are no saved rentals and provides a control to start a search based on this wishlist context.
event_handlers: onWishlistLoaded: If the wishlist-loader reports zero items, render the empty state instead of the rental list.
onClickSearchFromWishlist: Trigger the wishlist-derived-search component to open or focus, passing along the Wishlist and any template-related URL parameters.

components.functionality: Builds and manages a search template derived from the current wishlist and URL parameters, optionally lets the user override the destination, and initiates a new search by calling startNewSearch. Can also show a preview of returned results and provide navigation to the full search results page.
event_handlers: onOpen: Initialize the search template using data from the current Wishlist and items (for example, deriving a destination from their destination_id values) and reading any destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and pets parameters from the wishlist.html URL.

onChangeTemplateField: When the user edits date or guest fields (if present), update the in-memory search template and optionally reflect them back into the URL parameters to maintain state on reload.
onClickDestinationInput: Display the destination-autocomplete component to allow users to search and select a Destination.
onDestinationSelected: Receive a selected Destination from the destination-autocomplete component, update the template’s destinationId and destinationName, and update corresponding URL parameters.

onSubmitSearchTemplate: Validate that destinationId, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and pets are present in the template; if complete, call startNewSearch with these values (mapping adults to adultsCount, children to childrenCount, pets to petsCount). On success, store the returned SearchContext and results in local state for preview.
onPreviewResultsChanged: When new results are returned from startNewSearch, pass them to the search-results-preview-list component to render.

onClickViewAllResults: Use the current template or SearchContext to navigate to search_results.html with URL parameters destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and pets sourced directly from SearchContext or the template; no additional API calls are required for that navigation.

components.functionality: Provides an input field for selecting a Destination by name, using getDestinationSuggestions to fetch matching destinations as the user types and returning the chosen Destination to the parent search component.
event_handlers: onInputChange: After each user input change (with debouncing), call getDestinationSuggestions with the current input text as the query parameter and update the suggestion list with the returned Destination array.
onSuggestionClick: When the user selects a suggestion, emit the chosen Destination object to the wishlist-derived-search component via an onDestinationSelected event and close the suggestion list.
onBlur: Hide the suggestion list when the input loses focus, without altering the already selected destination if there is one.

components.functionality: Displays a preview list of RentalProperty results returned by startNewSearch, allows opening full listings, and provides controls to save any result into the current wishlist.
event_handlers: onResultsLoaded: When wishlist-derived-search provides a new results array from startNewSearch, render a result card for each RentalProperty.
onClickOpenListingFromResult: Navigate to listing.html with rentalId set to the selected RentalProperty.id.

onClickSaveResultToWishlist: For a selected result, call saveRentalToWishlist with rentalPropertyId from the RentalProperty and wishlistId from the current Wishlist. On success, either notify the wishlist-rental-list to append a corresponding item or update the UI state of the result card to reflect that it is saved.
onSaveResultError: If saveRentalToWishlist returns success=false, show an error state for that result card and do not alter the wishlist.

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and writing of URL query parameters for the wishlist detail page, ensuring sort, viewMode, and any search-template parameters remain synchronized with in-memory component state and are preserved across reloads and deep links.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse the wishlist.html URL for wishlistId (required) and optional parameters such as sort, viewMode, destinationId, destinationName, checkIn, checkOut, adults, children, and pets; expose these values to other components on initialization.

onStateChange: When components like wishlist-rental-list or wishlist-derived-search change high-level state (for example, sort option or destination override), update the browser URL query parameters to reflect the new state without reloading the page.
onUrlChange: Listen for browser navigation events that change query parameters (such as back/forward actions) and update dependent components so their state matches the URL.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered content container; card width suggests using 3–4 columns on desktop with large outer gutters.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow. Above this page’s content, the global green contact bar and logo/nav already occupy full width. The Wishlist Detail content begins immediately below the nav with a centered main content container. Within that container: the header-user-summary sits at the top, followed by a slim wishlist-summary-bar, then the wishlist-derived-search panel. Beneath these controls, the wishlist-rental-list runs vertically down the left side, composed of stacked wishlist-item-card components. Supporting states (loader, empty state, removal modal, autocomplete, and search-results preview) overlay or replace portions of this vertical flow without disturbing the overall hierarchy.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow. Above this page’s content, the global green contact bar and logo/nav already occupy full width. The Wishlist Detail content begins immediately below the nav with a centered main content container. Within that container: the header-user-summary sits at the top, followed by a slim wishlist-summary-bar, then the wishlist-derived-search panel. Beneath these controls, the wishlist-rental-list runs vertically down the left side, composed of stacked wishlist-item-card components. Supporting states (loader, empty state, removal modal, autocomplete, and search-results preview) overlay or replace portions of this vertical flow without disturbing the overall hierarchy.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A max-width centered content container (approx. 1120–1200px) sits within a full-width white canvas. The header-user-summary, wishlist-summary-bar, and wishlist-derived-search collectively use the top 20–25% of the visible content height on first load. The wishlist-rental-list and its cards dominate the remaining vertical space. On desktop, each wishlist-item-card occupies roughly 30–40% of the content width, aligned to the left columns of an implied 12-column grid (about 4 columns used, 8 columns left mostly as airy whitespace to the right). Vertical separation between major sections is around 32–48px, and between cards around 24–32px, following the spacious, airy style.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low. Each section has generous white space above and below. Cards have 16–24px internal padding, large imagery, and minimal text. Controls (summary bar, derived search, autocomplete) are visually light and compact, never crowding the cards. When empty, the wishlist-empty-state uses a large, open area with ample margins, echoing a lifestyle/marketing feel rather than a cramped dashboard.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mimics the mockup’s strong vertical, slightly left-biased F-pattern. After the global contact/nav bars, the eye hits the header-user-summary (wishlist name), then moves down to the wishlist-summary-bar and the derived search controls. From there, attention slides left to the primary column of wishlist-item-cards: each card’s large photo grabs attention first, then the title and price below. Right-side whitespace remains mostly empty, reinforcing focus on the left column and giving a relaxed, resort-like breathing space. Overlays like modals and loader states temporarily take over the focal point at the center of the viewport.

layout.overall_description: The Wishlist Detail page sits beneath the existing full-width contact strip and logo/navigation bar. Immediately below the nav, a centered main content container stretches across the middle of the screen, leaving generous white gutters on both sides. At the top of this container, a left-aligned header area announces the wishlist name and user context, with plenty of padding and negative space. Just under it, a slim summary bar runs across most of the container width, showing key counts and acting as a subtle horizontal anchor. A compact, card-like derived search panel follows, aligned to the left and occupying roughly a third of the container’s width, inviting the user to launch a new search from this wishlist. Below this control area, the content resolves into a tall, relaxed column of property cards: each card sits along the left 30–40% of the container width, featuring a wide, edge-to-edge image on top and a simple text block with title and price beneath, separated from the next card by generous vertical spacing. The right side of the content area remains mostly open, with only the white background and faint grid hinting at potential future cards or alternate layouts, preserving the airy, editorial feel. Overlays like the loader and removal confirmation modal appear centered over this layout with a subtle dimming of the background, while inline elements like autocomplete suggestions and search preview lists open directly beneath their triggers, aligned to the left, without breaking the overall vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-user-summary sits at the top of the page’s main content area, directly below the global logo/navigation bar. It spans the full width of the centered content container (approximately 80–90% of the browser width on desktop, capped around 1120–1200px), but its content is left-aligned, leaving comfortable whitespace on the right. The block has generous top and bottom padding (24–32px) to create a distinct header zone separate from the navigation above and the wishlist-summary-bar below. The wishlist name appears prominently on the left, with any auxiliary elements (e.g., owner info, share actions, edit name) arranged horizontally to its right but still within the left half of the container, preserving the left-weighted F-pattern. There is about 32px of vertical space separating this header from the wishlist-summary-bar that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the normal document flow, anchored to the top of the main content container and scrolling away with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-loader appears only while data is being fetched. It overlays the main content area starting just below the global navigation, covering the full width of the centered content container and extending down to fill the initial viewport height where the wishlist content will appear. Visually, it can present as either a semi-transparent white panel with subtle skeleton card shapes aligned to the left column where wishlist-item-cards will later appear, or as a centered spinner within that same region. Margins to the global header are preserved, maintaining the sense of space. The loader respects the left-biased layout by placing any skeletal card placeholders in the same approximate 30–40% width column on the left, leaving lighter activity or empty space on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the wishlist content region while active. Positioned relative to the main content container; prevents interaction with underlying wishlist elements until loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-summary-bar sits immediately below the header-user-summary, separated by roughly 24–32px of vertical space. It is a low-height horizontal bar spanning most of the content container width (around 90–100% of the container), with its primary information aligned to the left. Internally, it has slim vertical padding (12–16px) and 16–24px horizontal padding. Within this bar, key stats like number of rentals, total guests, or last updated date are arranged in a left-to-right manner, but confined to the left two-thirds of the bar to keep the right side visually light. A subtle bottom border or soft shadow can visually distinguish the bar from the white background while maintaining the minimal, relaxed look. There is about 24px of vertical space separating this bar from the wishlist-derived-search component below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normally scrolls with the page. Optionally, on taller lists, it can become a sticky element pinned to the top of the viewport just beneath the global navigation, maintaining visibility of high-level wishlist stats while the user scrolls through the cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-rental-list forms the main vertical column of saved rentals and begins below the control zone (wishlist-summary-bar and wishlist-derived-search), separated from those controls by approximately 32–48px to emphasize hierarchy. On desktop, the list is visually anchored to the left side of the content container and occupies roughly 30–40% of the container width (about 4 of 12 grid columns), leaving the remaining 60–70% as open, breathable whitespace to the right. Each child wishlist-item-card is stacked vertically within this column, with 24–32px vertical spacing between cards. The list extends downward, often beyond the fold, reinforcing a scroll-based exploration of properties. The right-side whitespace remains mostly untouched, framing the list and supporting the editorial-lifestyle feel rather than a dense multi-column grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A standard vertical list that scrolls with the page. Its width and left alignment respond to breakpoints: on smaller screens, it can expand to nearly full width while preserving vertical stacking, but on larger screens it remains a single, left-anchored column as per the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each wishlist-item-card is a vertically oriented card aligned to the left edge of the wishlist-rental-list column. On desktop, the card width is approximately 30–40% of the main content container (spanning about 4 of 12 grid columns), with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. The card’s top portion is dominated by a full-width property photo with a 16:9 or similar aspect ratio that reaches the card’s horizontal edges and sets the visual tone. Beneath the image, a white text block occupies the remaining card area with 16–24px padding: the property title is left-aligned at the top, followed by secondary details (location, number of beds) and the price line anchored toward the lower portion. The card corners are softly rounded, and a light shadow lifts it subtly off the background, matching the resort-like, airy style. Vertically, each card is separated from the next by a 24–32px gap, creating a clear rhythm as the user scrolls down the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the wishlist-rental-list. Responds to container width changes but maintains a single-column stacking behavior. When hovered or focused, the card may slightly elevate or strengthen its shadow to provide feedback without breaking the relaxed aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The removal-confirmation-modal appears at the center of the viewport when a user chooses to remove a rental from the wishlist. It floats above all content with a full-screen semi-transparent backdrop that softly dims the page while still allowing the underlying header and cards to be vaguely visible, preserving context. The modal itself is a compact, rounded-rectangle card roughly 30–40% of the viewport width on desktop, capped to keep it comfortably small. It is vertically centered with ample white space around it, and includes 24–32px internal padding. The confirmation message is left-aligned inside the modal, with primary and secondary action buttons aligned horizontally at the bottom right of the modal’s content area. The overall look matches the card style of the rest of the page: white background, light shadow, rounded corners, avoiding heavy borders or dense content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay anchored to the center of the viewport with a full-screen scrim. It remains in place while the user scrolls the underlying content (if allowed), effectively capturing focus until the action is confirmed or canceled.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-empty-state replaces the wishlist-rental-list region when there are no saved rentals. It appears below the header-user-summary and wishlist-summary-bar, occupying the central vertical section of the content container. Unlike the left-anchored cards, this state is more centered to convey a balanced, hero-like message: an illustration or icon appears near the center, with a concise headline and supporting text stacked vertically beneath it. The block spans roughly 50–60% of the content container’s width and is centered horizontally, with generous padding around the content (32–48px) and even larger vertical whitespace separating it from the header area above and the bottom of the viewport. A primary call-to-action button (e.g., “Start a search”) is centered under the text. The empty state is intentionally spacious and minimal, echoing a lifestyle marketing hero rather than a dense notification.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the wishlist-rental-list in the normal document flow when the list is empty. It scrolls with the page, but on typical screen sizes, its height and vertical padding keep it mostly within the initial viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-derived-search component sits between the wishlist-summary-bar and the wishlist-rental-list, separated from each by approximately 24–32px of vertical spacing. It appears as a compact, card-like panel anchored to the left side of the content container, occupying about 35–45% of the container width (roughly 4–5 grid columns). Inside, there is a brief explanation text at the top, followed by key controls such as a prominent button to ‘Start a search from this wishlist’ and optionally a small inline summary of pre-filled filters. The panel has 16–24px internal padding, rounded corners, and a subtle shadow consistent with other cards. Its right side aligns with the card column, and the remaining space to the right is left intentionally empty, keeping the layout visually light and focused on the left column as the main interaction zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the vertical flow. On narrow screens, it expands toward full width but remains positioned above the wishlist-rental-list, preserving the top-down progression from context to controls to listings.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The destination-autocomplete input lives inside the wishlist-derived-search panel (or a closely related search-control area) and is aligned to the left within that card. It spans approximately 60–80% of the panel’s width on desktop, with a minimum width that makes it feel substantial but not full-bleed. The input field is horizontally oriented with a light outline or bottom border, rounded corners, and 12–16px internal padding to match the minimal aesthetic. It sits below any explanatory text and above the primary search button, separated by 8–16px vertical gaps. When focused, its left edge aligns with the panel’s padding, ensuring a clean vertical alignment with text and other controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the wishlist-derived-search card, participating in normal layout flow. When active, it anchors the search-results-preview-list immediately below it, aligned to its left edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-results-preview-list appears immediately below the destination-autocomplete input when the user types, maintaining left alignment with the input field. It is a dropdown-style panel whose width matches the autocomplete field (or is slightly wider, but still within the boundaries of the wishlist-derived-search panel). The panel has a white background, subtle shadow, and rounded corners, visually consistent with other cards. Inside, suggestions are listed vertically with 8–12px vertical padding between items and 12–16px horizontal padding on each side. The panel’s height is capped so that 4–6 items are visible at once; additional items scroll inside the panel rather than expanding beyond the comfortable area. The dropdown visually overlaps the space that would otherwise be empty within the search panel area, without intruding into the wishlist-rental-list on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely or relatively positioned just below the destination-autocomplete input, floating above the underlying content within the wishlist-derived-search area. It appears and disappears in response to input focus and text changes, without affecting the overall page layout height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component is non-visual and does not occupy visible space on the page. Conceptually, it sits behind the scenes of the entire Wishlist Detail layout, listening to changes in wishlist filters, selected rentals, and derived search parameters, and keeping the browser URL in sync. It ensures that reloading or sharing the URL preserves the current scroll position and filter state, but it does so without introducing any UI elements, borders, or spacing of its own.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, purely behavioral component. It has no spatial footprint and does not affect the layout flow; it operates at the application state level, associated with the page as a whole rather than a specific on-screen region.

name: About
title: About
description: The About page loads dynamic information about the Hawaii vacation rental service (mission, specialization, trust elements, and how it works) and lets users start a new rental search via the global header search with destination suggestions and booking search initialization.

core_features: Load and render About-page content (title, body content, and highlight points) using the getAboutPageContent interface.

Display header user summary information (whether a profile exists, profile name, default wishlist rental count) using the getHeaderUserSummary interface to reinforce trust and continuity with the rest of the site.
Provide a global header search form on the About page that allows users to specify destination, dates, and guest counts, using getDestinationSuggestions for destination autocomplete.
On header search submission, initialize a new rental search using startNewSearch, then navigate to search_results.html with URL parameters for destination, dates, and guests.
Support internal navigation between informational sections on the About page (e.g., mission, how it works, trust elements) by reading and updating a section query parameter in the URL.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – View About information and trust highlights:
1. User navigates to about.html via header or footer navigation.
2. On page load, the client calls getHeaderUserSummary to populate header user information.
3. In parallel, the client calls getAboutPageContent to retrieve the About page title, bodyHtml, and highlights.
